Ladies & Gentlemen;

I have not yet succeeded in
making up for the time lost by my not
having overlooked the state of my dia-
grams at {add}my{/add}{del}the{/del} Second Lecture. My last lec-
ture, according to the programme, should have
been about Firstness, Secondness, and Third-
ness, and tonight about the gamma graphs.
But I was able to say very little about these
categories, which it is indispensible to con-
sider a little further in order to render the
gamma graphs intelligible; and without
the gamma graphs I should not be able
to make myself understood about Mul-
titude, Infinity, and Continuity, {del}one of the{/del}

subjects concerning which the thought of our
generation has made {add}decidedly{/add} the most extraordinary
and indisputable advances that it has made
in any direction. Besides, the {del}Doctrine of the{/del} three categories
is a matter of supreme interest.

Very early in my studies of logic, before
I had really been devoting myself to it more
than four or five years, it became quite mani-
fest to me that this science was in a bad
condition, entirely unworthy of the general
state of intellectual development of our
age; and in consequence of this, every
other branch of philosophy except ethics,
— for {add}it was already clear that{/add} psychology was a special science and
no part of philosophy,— was in a similar

disgraceful state. About that time, – say
the date of Mansell's Prolegomena Logica,
Logic touched bottom. {del}Mere degraded it could
not become.{/del} there was no room for it to become
more degraded. It had been sinking steadily
{del}and not alone{/del} {add}relatively{/add} to the advance of physical science
by no means slowly from the time of the
revival of learning, – say from the date of
the last fall of Constantinople. One important
addition to the subject had been made {add}only{/add} in the
eighteenth century, the Doctrine of Chances.
But this had not come from the professed
logicians, who knew nothing about it. Whewell,
it is true, had been doing some fine work; but
it was not of a fundamental character. De,

4

Morgan and Boole had {del}found{/del} {add}laid{/add} the foundations
for modern exact logic, but they {del}had hardly{/del} {add}can hardly{/add}
be said to have begun the erection of the edifice
itself. Under these circumstances, I naturally
opened the dusty {del}volumes{/del} {add}folios{/add} of the scholastic
doctors. Thought generally was, of course, in
a somewhat low condition under the
Plantagenets. You can appraise it very well
by the impression that Dante, Chaucer,
MarcoPolo, {del}and{/del} Froissart, and the great cathe-
drals make upon us. But logic, {del}[unclear: as is]{/del} {add}relatively to{/add}
the general condition of thought, was mar-
vellously exact and critical. They can tell
us nothing concerning methods of reasoning
since their own reasoning was puerile; but

{add}their discussions of all{/add}
their analyses of thought and {del}all that part{/del}
{add}Those questions of{/add}
of logic that almost trenched upon metaphy-
sics were very instructive as well as very
good discipline in that subtle kind of think-
ing that is required in logic.

In the days of which I am speaking, the
age of Robert of Lincoln, Roger Bacon, Sir
Thomas Aquinas, and Duns Scotus, the ques-
tion of nominalism and realism was regarded
as {del}all{/del} definitively and conclusively settled in
favor of realism. You know what the question
was, It was whether laws and general types
are figments of the mind or are real. If
this be understood to mean whether there
really are any laws and types, {add}strictly speaking{/add} it is a question

of metaphysics and not of logic. But as a first
step toward its solution, it is proper to ask
whether, granting that our common-sense
beliefs are true, the analysis of the meaning
of those beliefs shows that, according to those
beliefs, laws and types are objective or
subjective, this is {del}more properly{/del} a question
of logic rather than of metaphysics, - and as
soon as this is answered the reply to the other
question immediately follows after.
Notwithstanding a great outbreak
of nominalism in the fourteenth century
which was connected with politics, the
nominalists being generally opposed to the
excessive powers of the pope and in favor

of civil government, {del}[illegible]{/del} a connection that
lent to the philosophical doctrine a factitious
following, the Scotists, who were realists,
were in most places the {add}predominant{/add} {del}dominant{/del} party, and
{del}[illegible]{/del} retained possession of the universities. At
the revival of learning they stubbornly opposed the
new studies; and thus the word Duns, {add}the{/add} {del}form{/del}
{del}proper{/del} name of their master, came to mean an
{del}the{/del} adversary of learning. The word originally
further implied that the person so called was
a master of subtle thought with which the
humanists were unable to cope. But in
another generation the disputations by
which that power of thought was kept in
training had lost their {del}then{/del} {add}liveliness{/add}; and the

8

consequence was that Scotism died {add}quasi{/add} when the
strong Scotists died. It was a mere change
of fashion. The humanists were weak thinkers.
Some of them no doubt might have been trained to
be strong thinkers; but they had not severe training
in thought. All their energies went to writing a
classical language and an artistic style of
expression. They went to the ancients for their
philosophy; and {add}mostly{/add} took up the three easiest,
of the ancient sects of philosophy, Epicurean-
ism, stoicism, and scepticism. Epicure-
anism was a doctrine extremely like
that of John Stuart Mill. The Epicureans alone
{add}of the {add}later{/add} ancient schools{/add}
believed in inductive reasoning, which they
grounded upon the uniformity of nature, al-

9

though they made the uniformity of nature to
consist in somewhat different characters from
those Stuart Mill emphasizes, Like Mill the
Epicureans were extreme nominalists. The Stoics
advocated the flatest materialism, which nobody
{del}were flat materialists, such as are no longer{/del}
any longer has any need of denying since the
{add}new invention{/add} {del}Doctrine{/del} of Monism enables a man to be,
perfectly materialist in substance, and as
idealistic as he likes in mode, Of course
the Stoics {add}could not but be,{/add} {del}were nominalists{/del}. They took no
stock in inductive reasoning. They {add}held{/add} {del}considered{/del}
it to be a transparent fallacy. The sceptics
of the renaissance were something like
the agnostics of the generation now
passing away, except that that they were

10

much further. Our agnostics contented them-
selves with declaring everything beyond ordi-
nary generalizations of experience to be
unknowable, while the sceptics did not,
think any {del}such{/del} scientific knowledge of any
description to be possible. If you turn over
the pages, for example, of Cornelius Agrippa's
{del}book{/del} 'De vanitate scientiarum,' you
will find he takes up every science in suc-
cession, arithmetic, geometry, mechanics,
optics and after examination pronounces
each to be altogether
as beyond the power of {add}the{/add} human mind.
Of course, therefore, as far as they believed
in anything at all, the sceptics were
nominalists, In short, there was a tidal

11

wave of nominalism.  Descartes, was a no-
minalist.  Locke and all his followers,
Berkeley, Hartley, Hume, and even Reid,
were nominalists.  Leibniz was an
{del}extreme{/del} nominalist, and Remusat who
has lately made an attempt to repair the
edifice of Leibnizian monadology, does so
by cutting away every part which leans
at all toward realism.  Kant was a no-
malist, although his philosophy & paper
been rendered compact, more cons of science

as he certainly would have read Abbot in the
read Scotus.  Hegel was [unclear: duction?] to his book
realistic yearnings.  Then, showed on the con-

all modern philosophy of every {del}kind{/del} sect
has been nominalistic, for a {add}long notice{/add} {del}review{/del} of Frazer's
Berkeley, in the North American Review for
October 1871, I declared for realism. I have
since very carefully {add}and{/add} {del}&{/del} thoroughly revised my
philosophical opinions more than half a
dozen times, and have modified them more or
less on most topics; but I have never been
able to think differently on that question of
nominalism and realism. In that paper
I acknowledged that the tendency of science
has been toward nominalism; but the
late Dr. Francis Ellingwood Abbot in the
very remarkable introduction to his book
entitled "Scientific Theism," showed on the con-

{del}[unclear: thing]{/del}, quite conclusively, that science has always
been at heart Realistic, and always must be,
so; and upon comparing his writing with
mine, {del}one{/del} {add}it is{/add} easily seen that these features of
nominalism which I pointed out in science
are merely superficial and transient.
The heart of the dispute lies in this.
{add}The{/add}
{del}All{/del} modern philosophers, + one and all,
unless Schelling be an exception,—
recognize but one mode of being, the
being of an individual thing or fact,
the being which consists in the object's
{add}out a place for itself in{/add}
{del}[unclear: first] crowding strongly into{/del} the universe,
so to speak, and reacting by brute force
of fact, against all other things. I call that

14

Existence. {del}[illegible]{/del} Aristotle {add}on the other hand{/add} whose system, like
all the greatest systems, was evolutionary,
recognizes besides an embryonic kind
of being, like the being of a tree in its seed,
or like the being of a future contingent event,
depending on how a man shall decide
to act. In a few passages Aristotle seems
to have a dim [unclear: aperçue?] of a third mode
of being in the entelechy. The embryonic being
for Aristotle was the being {del}of{/del} {add}he calls{/add} Matter, which
is alike in all things, and which in the
course of its development took on Form.
Form * is an element having a different
mode of being. The whole philosophy
of the scholastic doctors is an attempt

15

to mould this doctrine of Aristotle into har-
mony with christian {del}ideas{/del}. {add}Truth.{/add} This {add}harmony{/add} the different
doctors attempted to bring about in different
ways. But all the realists agree in reversing
the order of Aristotle’s evolution by making
the Form come first, and the Individuation
of that form come later. Thus, they too
recognized two modes of being; but
they were not the two modes of being of
Aristotle.

My view is that there are three
modes of being, & {del}maintain{/del} {add}hold{/add} that we
can directly observe them in elements
of whatever is at anytime before the
mind in any way. They are the being

16

of positive qualitative possibility, the
being of actual fact, and the being of
law that will govern facts in the future.
Let us begin with considering actuality,
and try to make out just what it consists
in. If I ask you what the actuality of an
event consists in, you will tell me that it
consists in its happening then and there. The
specifications then and there involve all its
relations to other existents. The actuality {add}of the event{/add} seems
to be in {del}relationship{/del} {add}its relations{/add} to the universe of existents.
A court may issue injunctions and judgments
against me & I not care a snap of my finger for
{del}[unclear: and think them idle vapor]{/del}
them. But when I feel the sheriff's hand on my
shoulder, I shall begin to have a sense of

actuality. Actuality is something brute. There
is no reason in it. I instance putting your
shoulder against a door & trying to force it
open against an unseen, silent, and un-
known resistance. We have a {del}double{/del} {add}two sided{/add} conscious-
ness of effort and resistance, which seems
true to come tolerably near to a pure sense of
actuality. On the whole, I think we have
here a mode of being of one thing which
consists in how a second object is. I call
that Secundness.

Besides this, there are two modes of being
that I call Firstness & Thirdness. Firstness
is the mode of Being which consists in its
subject’s being positively such as it is regardless

18

of aught else. That can only be a possibility  
For as long as things do not act upon one  
another there is no sense or meaning  
in saying that they have any being, {del}as{/del}  
unless it be that they are such in them-  
selves that they may {del}go{/del} {add}perhaps{/add} come into  
relation with others. The mode of being  
a redness, before anything in the uni-  
verse was yet red, was nevertheless a  
positive qualitative possibility. And  
redness in itself {del}is for{/del} {add}even if it be embodied is{/add}  
something positive {add}and{/add} sui generis. That I  
call Firstness. We {add}naturally{/add} attribute Firstness to  
outward objects, that is we suppose they have  
capacities in themselves which may or may

not to {add}the{/add} actualized, which may or may not
ever be actualized, although we can know

Now for Thirdness. Five minutes of our
waking life will hardly pass without our
making some kind of prediction; and in the
majority of cases these predictions are ful-
filled in the event. Yet a prediction is essen-
tially of a general nature, and cannot be
ever be completely fulfilled. To say that a

fulfilled, is to say that the future events

law. If a pair of dice turns up sixes five

{add}might{/add}

running. But that would not afford the

would turn up sixes the next time. If the

body ever dreamed of denying that what is

form to it or not. If they will, {add}your{/add} adjective
“mere” seems to be ill-placed. A rule to which
future events have a tendency to conform

Gaius Julius Caesar

{add}the collection{/add}
{del}Julius Caesar{/del} composed of Julius Caesar, of
this piece of chalk, and the replica of Caesar's
name that I have put on the board is a single
object. Thus a collection has a mixed nature.
It shares in part the nature of a substantive
possibility, like a quality; while in part it shares
the nature of what we call in philosophy {del}first{/del} a substance
that is an individual object existing {add}in{/add} {del}by{/del} an act
of brute {del}comp{/del}{add}reaction{/add}. Some of the qualities of collec-
tions are like the qualities of first substances and
consist in what is true of its members. Thus, when
we say the United States has free government, we
mean, that {add}all{/add} {del}many{/del} those states are largely inhabited
by voters whose majority determines who is to

{del}fill the offices,{/del} together with other such facts.

When we say that a people is turbulent, we mean
{del}that some of its{/del} {del}a considerable part{/del} {add}many men{/add} {del}of its people{/del} who are members
of it forcibly oppose other members who are a
part of it. On the other hand a collection also
has qualities which are similar to those
general respects in which {del}one{/del} qualities agree
and differ. Chief among {del}these potential{/del} {add}these{/add}
respects is multitude, or maniness. Let me
repeat that I never use the word multitude
to mean a large collection. {del}but in{/del} I invari-

Ladies and Gentlemen

At the end of the last lecture I gave a rule for
positively ascertaining whether a graph is alpha-
possible or not. It also {del}determines{/del} {add}furnishes{/add} a description of
the state of things in which it is possible. I said I
supposed it was not the best possible rule; but it is
reasonably speedy. Considering that the system of
existential graphs is not intended to be used as a
calculus, it satisfies all requirements.

{del}then we come to beta possibility, it is impracti-{/del}

{del}enormous in every but the simplest cases.{/del}

The idea of that rule was double correspond-
ing to two different distinctions among the six

4

fundamental alpha permissible operations. Namely
of these six, there are three that complicate the
graph and three that simplify it. The three that
complicate it are insertion, iteration, and the {add}making{/add}
{del}place{/del} of a double cut. The three that simplify it are erasure,
deiteration, and the cancelling of a double cut. {add}There{/add}
{add}there{/add} are of the six operations, two only that are not re-
versible, that is which {add}may not{/add} {del}cannot{/del} be undone as well
as done. These are erasure and insertion. Now my
idea in drawing up the rule was in the first place, to
define to myself precisely {add}on what occasions{/add} {del}for{/del} the complicative ope-
rations might be indispensable in ascertaining
whether a graph was possible or not, and to restrict
their virtual employment to these cases, and in the
second place, so to modify the non-reversible operations

as to render them reversible.
The {add}method{/add}

mere combinations, or else you {del}[unclear: will have]{/del} {add}may find{/add} a
difficulty in apprehending their true nature.
Take the relation of giving. The {del}giver{/del} {add}donor{/add} gives a {del}gift{/del} {add}donation{/add}
{del}to a givee.{/del} {add}the donatee.{/add} Giving is not a mere physical
fact. It does not consist in the giver's throwing

70

away the object and {add}that object{/add} {del}[unclear: to imp]{/del} {add}striking{/add} the other. Even if
The {del}[unclear]{/del} thrower had aimed his shot at the other,
{add}that aiming{/add}
it would have been an act of thought. Even if
he had thrown it without any reference to the
other and it had merely hit {add}that{/add} {del}the{/del} other by chance,
{add}which would be thus{/add}
the regarding these two events merely casually
connected as one act, would be a creation of thought
But genuine giving is not necessarily accompanied
by any physical act except that of the expression
of an idea. “I give it.” Its essence lies in the trans-
fer of the right of property. Now right of property is
a matter of law; and law is a mental affair.
Take the relation between two facts making up
a whole and that whole. That is a {del}[unclear]{/del} {add}triadic{/add} relation.
Now either these parts make the whole by being {del}[unclear]{/del} {add}brought{/add}
together or otherwise modified or else their mere,

72

being constitutes the whole. In the former case the whole
is an ens rationis, — a thing of thought. For
an ens rationis is by definition, that whose being
consists in some fact about other things. Now the
whole consists in the fact {del}that{/del} {add}of the{/add} necessary modification
of the parts {del}[unclear: draw?]{/del} {add}in order{/add} to constitute it, these parts not
{add}being{/add} {del}in{/del} by their mere being, {del}being{/del} identical with that whole.
If on the other hand, the parts are not brought together
and there is nothing at all true of them to make them
into the whole; as when we consider the procession
of the equino[unclear: xes?] and the American eagle as constituti-
{del}ting{/del} {add}a{/add} whole, the purely mental, not to say fanciful,
character of the whole comes out all the more plainly.
You can multiply examples for yourselves. {del}Con-
ously every thought involves a triadic relation{/del}

74

Conversely, every thought, involves the idea of a
{del}triadic relation; For every thought involves the{/del}
idea of a sign. Now a sign is {del}an object which{/del} {add}a thing related to an object{/add}
{add}and{/add} determining in the interpreter an interpreting sign
of the same object. It involves the relation between
sign, interpreting sign, and object. There is a
{del}two{/del} three-fold distinction between signs, which
is not in the least psychological in its nature,
but is purely logical, and is of the utmost
importance in logic. Three kinds of elements
can be discerned in {del}every object which{/del} {add}whatever{/add} comes before
{del}appearance{/del} The mind. {del}First{/del} The first is the element of Quality,
{add}and including all the suchness {unclear: possibilities}{/add}
such for example, as Red; {del}It is that element{/del}
of the idea in itself, as mere ideal suchness. It is that
which is such as it is, positively, and {del}[illegible]{/del} quite
irrespective of anything else. I call this element

76

the element of Firstness. {del}the second element{/del} {add}Besides its direct presence we{/add}
attribute to things out of us natures of their own
{add}actual reactions,{/add}
irrespective of {del}other things{/del} The second element
is directly experienced in our {add}sense{/add} of their here,
in our sense of present fact, which is the experi-
ence of actual reaction with a non-ego. It consists
{add}something is{/add}
in being that which another makes it to be, here
and now. I {del}call{/del} {add}give to{/add} this element of doubleness {del}by{/del} the
name of Secondness. Besides, directly experiencing
it, we regard outward objects as compelling one
another, as {del}our{/del} {add}exerting{/add} force upon one another. The third
element is the moulding of brute reactions {del}to{/del} {add}into{/add}
conformity to {del}the force{/del} ideas. It is the growth
outward expression of {del}available{/del} {add}or thought{/add} which can only be
thought in the expression, this expression con-
sisting of a bending of reactions to the

78

form of the idea. But, dear me, what a fearfully
abstruse matter I am making of {del}that{/del} which
is the easiest thing in the world to understand,
the nature of a {add}general{/add} sign. Yes, strictly speaking
the very easiest of all things to understand; for
what we call understanding anything else
merely consists in seeing that it is of the
nature of a sign. Every little child under-
stand it perfectly. But as he grows up and
loses his {add}gift{/add} {del}faculty{/del} of language, it becomes more
difficult; and the more he studies and cul-
tivates his mind the more inscrutable this
{add}which is the only comprehensible thing in the universe{/add}
simple business of a sign appears to him
To be. Ah, what says Wordsworth to the child?

80

Thou whose exterior semblance doth belie
Thy soul's immensity;
Thou best philosopher who yet dost keep
Thy heritage, thou eye among the blind,
That, deaf and silent, read'st the eternal deep,
Haunted for ever by the eternal Mind,—
Mighty prophet! Seer blest!
On whom those truths do rest
Which we are toiling all our lives to find.
Oh, it is difficult to have patience with people
who imagine that the action of blind force can explain
all the phenomena of this world, or even a single one
of them. What happens? I observe nature. I think
I detect a regularity, and I hazard a prediction.
Well, I am often wrong. But subsequently the

82

event turns out according to the prediction; and not,

the first time only, {add}nor{/add} the second, nor the tenth,

nor the hundredth, {del}the{/del} nor the thousandth,

but {del}not{/del} right straight along. And yet there are people,

{del}that will tell you that{/del} Laws are not real! The prediction

gets verified. That is the whole story, provided you

realize all that that means. Provided, for example, you

{del}[unclear: can]{/del} perceive that {add}the making of{/add} {del}to make a{/del} prediction is an

action; and that every action consists in making

a prediction. The air in my study is stuffy. I say to

myself, the window should be opened. Well, then I shall

have to open it. Then I shall have to cross the room.

Then I shall have to rise. And presently I find myself

crossing the room; & the window gets opened.

This case in its {add}most{/add} general features is just like the

84

{add}because the presence of
my body & its selfcontrol
was a large factor in it,{/add}

other. This is a prediction. If people would only dismiss
from their minds the {del}stupid{/del} notion that this
universe is ruled by brute force, considering
that predictions are verified & actions shaped to pur-
poses solely by the regularity of the reactions &
that regularity, relating as it does to what is
about to happen, is necessarily an affair of
thought, and certainly not the predictor’s thought
but the thought of God, they would then see,
what signs really amount to. This element
of our daily & hourly experience, the element
of the conformity of fact to thought, - this
element where being such as it is consists
in this that it {del}is so{/del} {add}has such{/add} reference to an
object independent of it is to bring a

third thing (the interpretation) into the same
triadic relation to that same object, -- this
character of a sign, the being an exponent
of thought, is what I call the element
of Thirdness in the phenomenon. [unclear: 18th?]

Now, I was saying, there are three kinds of
signs. the first kind consists of icons, which like
all signs are such only by virtue of being interpreted
as such, but {add}whose{/add} {del}which{/del} significant character which
causes them to be so interpreted is their {del}fact{/del} {add}possessing{/add}
a quality, in consequence of which they may be taken
as representative of anything that may happen to
exist that has that quality. Of course there are
no signs that are exclusively iconic. But a
geometrical diagram {add}for example,{/add} represents
say, a triangle, simply because it is like it,

The second class of signs consists of Indices, {add}whose{/add}  
significant character which causes them to be  
used as {del}indicated{/del} signs lies in {del}the{/del} a matter of {add}positive{/add} fact, the fact  
that they are really {del}connected{/del} {add}related{/add} correlates to the  
objects they denote. This {del}class{/del} {add}genus{/add} of signs has two  
species, of which the first consists of those that  
are merely connected with their objects and  
call attention to them, of which an example  
would be Bunker Hill Monument which its  
designer Horatio Greenough, declared he meant  
should say “Here!” merely that, and nothing  
more. The other species {del}are compounded of{/del} {add}consist of{/add}  
Indices of the first species which carry  
attached to them and {del}thus calling attention{/del}  
to them Icons of these same objects. Such is the  
weathercock which is not only connected

with the wind but points in the same direction.

The third class of signs are Symbols, which
{del}are{/del} not only, like all signs, function as such
only in being interpreted as such, but further
have for their special significant character
merely the certainty, based on some habit,
natural disposition, or convention, that they
will be {del}[illegible]{/del} understood in certain ways. Thus
{add}the{/add} {del}a{/del} word “man” bears not the smallest resemblance
to a man, nor is it {del}part{/del} {add}physically{/add} connected with
a man, nor otherwise sufficiently connected
to make that much reason for {del}interpreting it{/del} {add}using{/add}
{del}as{/del} a sign of a man; but which is certain to be
understood to mean a man and therefore is
an excellent sign of one. This genus of signs

Lowell Lectures of 1903.
Lecture III. 2nd Draught

Ladies & Gentlemen:
    I am going to speak to you concerning
multitude. By multitude I mean that quality of
a collection by virtue of which it is greater than
all those collections than which it is greater.
    A collection is a single object whose {del}ex ist{/del} {add}being{/add}
consists in the {del}being{/del} {add}existence{/add} of whatever {add}independent individuals{/add} may exist
of which a given {del}proposition{/del} {add}schema{/add} is true, these inde-
pendent individuals being called the members
of the collection.
    It is generally agreed among mathematical logi-
cians that the doctrine of multitude {del}which is possi-{/del}
{del}only speaking a logical doctrine, although under-{/del}
{del}esting in the mathematical side took it rise{/del}
{del}from the definition by Bernard Bolzano of being{/del}
greater than as {del}only applied to collections{/del}.
began from the definition by Bernard Bolzano
of the relation of a greater collection to a lesser
one. Bernard Bolzano was a catholic {del}logician{/del} {add}theologian{/add}
whose logic in 4 volumes was published in
1838. He was far too clearheaded to escape
persecution and his definition of a greater
collection only appeared in a posthumous
work published in 1851.

13

Before {del}giving{/del} that definition one or two {del}remarks{/del} remarks will
be useful. If anything is true, definitely and decidedly
true, that of which it is said to be true may be in
some sense a creation of the mind. Still, once
created, it must be in a measure independent of
thought, so that merely denying the truth of what
is asserted shall not destroy its truth. Otherwise,
it does not mean anything to say it is true.
Thus, you may imagine a blue horse. Afterward
it may seem to you more sensible to suppose
it was a bay horse. Still, all the thought in the
world cannot set Humpty Dumpty up. What
you did imagine was a blue horse. The Humpty Dumpty
of the old riddle is that freedom which thought has until
it comes to a decision.
{del}Now what is true{/del}
Now if you inform me of any truth, and I know
it already, there is no information. If it is some-
thing that I shall never have any further reason to
believe, {del}it does not concern me at all,{/del} you are
speaking of a universe with which I have no concern.
and What you say signified nothing to me. If it is genuine
information, it must amount to this, that whenever
and wherever in the future such and such {add}circum{/add}
stances occur, then I shall experience something.
I beg you to notice that any information which
ostensibly relates to the present condition of things
really signifies what the person addressed
will experience provided an opportunity occurs.

15

I emphasize this in order to prevent you from
thinking that because a truth related to what
can be done, it does not relate to the actual
state of things. Of course it does not say that
what can be done has been done; but in saying
what can be done it says precisely what
all genuine information {del}really{/del} about the present
state of things really limits itself to saying.
For instance, if I say that the fingers of my
hand are five, what I mean is that if I apply
the series vocables, one, two, three, and
so on to them, one by one, I shall come to a
stop with five. Now if anybody says these
cardinal numbers are mere creations of your
mind or are not even there, for they have
no more meaning than the vocables [unclear: Cleng?]
[unclear: Mleeng?], [unclear: Morny?], [unclear: Mli?], [unclear: Tusthad?], [unclear: Lana?], Bema.
[unclear: Strig?], I am obliged to grant that what he
says is true. But if he {add}goes on to urge{/add} {del}says that{/del}, such being
the case, my fingers are not really five until
they have been counted, I reply that {del}they{/del} {add}on the contrary{/add} their
being five now before they have been counted
is a fact of the most real kind of reality
I say of a piece of chalk that it is soft. If some-
body objects, “No! It can be scratched; but at
present, nobody is scratching it”, I say to
him “My dear sir, you forget that the purpose
of word is nothing but their purpose.”

{del}So if there{/del} So to say that there are 120 different
orders in which I can count my five fingers
is {add}not precisely{/add} the same as {del}to say{/del} that there really are now
120 different {add}[unclear: mediate?]{/add} relations in which the five
members stand to the five fingers. For {add}general{/add} relations
are in themselves mere possibilities. That is all
the being they have. It is all the being that general
relations ever can have; for the moment
they are actualized they cease in existence
to be general.
A collection, however, consists of simple
individuals which must exist.
Existence cannot, of its very nature, realize
all possibilities. .:.
I will now define the relation {add}of a greater collection{/add} to the lesser one;
That is to say, I state precisely the relation of this
idea {del}to other{/del} ideas.
One collection, say that of the As
[diagram: seven red capital As in a horizontal row]
is greater than another collection, say
that of the Bs
[diagram: five red capital Bs in a horizontal row]
if and only if there is no relation in which
every A stands to a B to which no other A
{del}relation{/del} is in this same relation.

When we pass to the problem of {del}[illegible]{/del} beta possibility,
that is, of formulating a rule for positively

Yet from this graph as a premiss no less than
ninety entirely independent conclusions can
be drawn, showing the misconception involved in
the popular expression, “the* conclusion from given”

part of the graph more than once. By
repeated making use of the same assertion, any

40

number of conclusions could be drawn. {del}According to{/del}

In the alpha-part of logic to which the ordinary text books
of logic are virtually confined, because although if the
{del}problem{/del} they consider were stated in graphical form,
there would be ligatures, yet they are only such as

This proposition has been a great puzzle to

Ladies and Gentlemen:

The alpha part of graphs [unclear: of]

The beta part of which I was able

a very intricate kind, and proposi-

4

has learned to think in beta graphs

of a person who has not that advan-
tage. His reasonings generally turns
upon the properties of the relations
of individual objects to one another.
But it is able to do nothing at all
with many ideas which we are
all perfectly familiar with. Gene-
rally speaking it is unable to reason

6

about abstractions. It cannot reason
for example about qualities nor about
relations as subjects to be reasoned
about. It cannot reason about ideas.
It is to supply that defect that the
gamma part of the subject has been
invented. But this gamma part is
still in its infancy. It will be many
years before my successors will be,
able to {del}give{/del} bring it to the perfection

8

[diagram: left margin bracket marked “over” with downward arrow]
Exact logic was started by Augustus
De Morgan in 1846, who produced a
great variety of necessary reasonings
entirely beyond the scope of the tra-
ditional logic, and in particular
made a considerable investigation of
the logic of relations, of syllogisms

definite syllogisms.
In 1847, George Boole, a great genius,

9

graphs; but he subsequently used it to

[diagram: marginal brace enclosing preceding passage, with small alpha-like mark]
The gamma part of graphs, in its
present condition, is characterized by

It is true that a line of identity is a graph;

of identity have a common point, is

Thus in place a sheet of assertion, we have
a book of separate sheets, tacked together
at points, if not otherwise connected. For
our alpha sheet, as a whole, represents
simply a universe of existent individuals, and the different {add}parts{/add} {del}points{/del} of

made concerning that universe. At the

conceived propositions which are not
realized; In these areas there may

which tho in the imaginary worlds

24

assertion itself, although this is
uncertain. You may regard the ordinary
blank sheet of assertion as a film upon
which there is {add}as it were{/add} an undeveloped photograph
of all the facts in the universe. I do not mean

sition is abstract and altogether of a

ask you to imagine {del}that{/del} all the true pro-

can only be in a continuum that we can

25

the continuity and connection of parts
being {del}[unclear: anywise]{/del} {add}[unclear: ever retained]{/add}. Of this continuum
the blank sheet of assertion may be ima-
gined to be a photograph. When we find

sheet, because we can imagine the
original continuum which is plastic
to be so deformed as to bring any {del}[illegible]{/del}
of propositions to any [illegible]

28.

places on the sheet we may choose. So far
I have called the sheet a photograph, so
as not to overwhelm you with all the
difficulties of the conception at once. But

An inference is not rendered invalid be-
cause its conclusion is false; it is only a
false conclusion following from true pre-
mises which is inconsistent with the validi-
ty of the reasoning. In necessary reason-
ing, at least, it is too much to expect that
the conclusion should come out true, irre-
spective of the truth of the premises; that would
be a magical logic. If it is possible for the
premises to be true while the conclusion is
false, the inference (supposing it necessary)
is invalid. But if this is not the case, the
inference must be allowed to be valid. That
is to say, the inference is valid, if in {del}every{/del}

1. Equivocation is when the deceit consists
in the doubtfulness of some one word having
divers significations.

2. Amphibology is when the double meaning
resides, not in a single word, but in a phrase.

3. Composition or conjunction is the joining
of things that should be severed. As, two and three
are odd and even, five is two and three; hence,
five is odd and even. It is impossible for a man
to sit and stand; John is sitting; therefore, John
cannot stand. Every animal is either rational or
irrational; but not every animal is rational; hence,
every animal is irrational.

4. Division. The opposite fault. All the planets
are seven; the sun and moon are planets;
hence, the sun and moon are seven.

5. Accent is a pern used sophistically.

being analogous in form are assumed to be

The doctrine of the quantified predicate is, strictly speaking, the doc-
trine that identity is the primary and fundamental relation expressed
by the copula of a proposition. To that doctrine I am thoroughly opposed.
The arguments of the advocates of this position are too shallow
to be of any service in helping us to the truth. Indeed, I do not remember
any formal argument in its favor except that of Sir William Hamilton,
which is that in logic that which is implicit in thought should be made
explicit in language. Now, that is said to be "implicit" in a proposi-
tion which can be necessarily inferred from that proposition. But the
logic of relatives shows that an innumerable multitude of propositions can
be inferred from any proposition whatever; and all of them cannot be
explicitly set forth. Sir William Hamilton's postulate would abolish
inference, except as a prelogical operation; but in that respect it
faithfully represents the doctrine of the quantified predicate itself. It
is the chief task of logic gradually to develop {del}rule{/del} that which is implicit
in thought and {del}formal{/del} step by step to make it explicit, or at least, to
show how to do so; and to begin by postulating that this is done, is
to pass by the chief purpose of logic.
But Hamilton may sometimes have confused his postulate, as
stated, with this: to set forth explicitly in the forms of logic that which
is necessarily explicit in the thought. Whether he really did fall into this
confusion, it is not easy, owing to the brevity of his papers, to determine.
This amended postulate, however, leaves {add}the{/add} quantified predicate just where
it was before; for the whole question is, what is necessarily explicit in thought?

2

The considerations which really actuate these exact logicians who
have virtually given in their adhesion to the doctrine of the quan-
tified predicate are, I apprehend, such as the following:
First, exact logic is to take mathematics for its guide. Now in
ordinary algebra equations (which are but expressions of the special
modification of identity which refers to quantity) are the leading
propositions.
Second, the majority of the fundamental formulae of algebraical
logic are equations; and those that are not most simply so expres-
sed may be, nevertheless, replaced by equations.
Third, the expression of logical formulae as equations enables
us to unite logical with numerical algebra, as one consistent
whole, with great advantage.
Fourth, definitions {del}which are{/del} {add}and other{/add} convertible propositions, wherever
they can be established in science, are of peculiar value; and the
mind is not satisfied until such discoveries are reached. Until
they are reached, therefore, it {add}mightily{/add} subserves the purpose of science, to
put our propositions into the form of equations, involving as they
must, in that imperfect stage of knowledge, an indication of
their imperfection.
Fifth, what has just been said of propositions is equally true
of inferences. [unclear: §?] A conclusion from which the colligated premisse
cannot be reinferred, is an imperfect conclusion; and its imper-
fection should be marked in the logical representation of it.

Z

as far as they go. But it is clear that they are not demonstrative; and
therefore it will be proper to postpone further study of them until
after the principal arguments upon the other side have been examined.

A distinction into two classes is generally vague. Reasoning
à priori is inferring a consequent from an antecedent. Reasoning
à posteriori is inferring an antecedent from its consequent. There is
clearly a third way of reasoning, which consists in inferring a mutual
connection between the several consequents of one antecedent; this is
induction.

It is important to distinguish three kinds of relation of antecedent
and consequence. The 1st is sequence in the object represented as
it is meant to be understood; as when I say "If the heart beats
the patient lives" where the sequence is from the life to the heart beating.
This is cause and effect or in a particular case a law, and reasoning
is with reference to this sequence demonstrated {del}subjectively{/del} {add}externally{/add} à priori, à posteriori
or inductive. The 2nd sequence lies in the representation in so far as
it is thought to be a representation. This is the sequence from the false to
the like in the above example. If A, then B; But B; hence A. In the
major premiss the sequence in the representation as it was taken to be a
representation was from B to A; as the reasoning shows. This sequence
is from reason to conclusion through argument. Reasoning is in refer-
ence to this sequence to be denominated internally à priori, à posteriori, or
inductive. The 3rd sequence lies in the representation as an image
in itself. This is the grammatical sequence from protasis to apodosis
through a principle. Reasoning in reference to it is to be denominat

Rules for the quantification of the predicate in conversion.

1 Affirmative predicates can only be quantified indefinitely.
The reason is simple. In the praejacent proposition we {del}[unclear: represent]{/del}

It has been said that {del}the common{/del} subjaccent has a peculiar
quantity "at least some." But this is not true. There are
only two quantities considered - Universal and Particular. We
do assert the particular, we do not assert the Universal. The
remark seems to contemplate "None" as a third quantity, but
this does not belong to the quantity of the subject. Thus if I
convert {del}some{/del} "All griffins are animals" into "some animals
are griffins" These some may as well be none as not. When
I say, "All griffins are animals" I not only do not say that grif-
fins are actual, I do not say that they are conceivable, or have
any quality whatever, by which animals could be qualified in order
to introduce a distinction between some and none. Even
All may be none. Some therefore {del}[illegible]{/del} contradicts
either all or none or both.

2 Negative predicates are to be quantified universally, &

Considerations of the Advantages of
Sir W. H's Analytic
[unclear: intended?] to show that mine has the same.
[horizontal rule]

1. His postulate of Logic - that we be allowed to state in language
what is contained in thought - is fully contained in my
definition of logic that it is the science of the forms
of representation,

But when he applies this postulate to proving the necessity of
a quantified predicate, on the ground that the predicate is quanti-
fied in thought, he is going beyond the bounds of logic; because
logic deals with thought only in so far as this is a representa-
tion of intuitions, and further only in so far as the parts of
a representation are connected or relevant; consequently
he must show not only that the predicate is quantified but also
that its quantification affects its relation to the subject.
He says that if I do not know {del}that{/del} {add}whether{/add} rose comes under the concept flower
{del}—{/del} whether it is equal to some part, or the whole, or superior to it
- I do not know the class flower, and cannot of course predi-
cate flower of rose. Now I think this reason is irrelevant.
It seems to me as like this: I cannot know a line without
knowing a solid, therefore in mathematics this is false to con-
sider lines by themselves and not as edges. My reply is that in

Logic we are not occupied with cognition or the modes
of cognition, but only with the forms of representation.
Now when I say a rose is a flower, the object of the repre-
sentation is the rose not the flower, and any consideration
of the limits of the flower except within the rose, is irrele-
vant to the representation. In short, Sir W. H's doctrine on
this point cannot be deduced from any postulate; it must
be deduced from some criterion of relevancy.

Another argument for Sir W.H. is that predication is nothing more nor
less than the expression of the relation of quantity in which a
notion stands to an individual or two notions to each other. If this
premiss were true, as understood, the conclusion would follow.
But I oppose to it the fact of universal grammar that every propo-
sition has a subject -- of which we speak through the predicate,
so that a predication is the representation of an object in a
particular relation; so that we do not {del}speak{/del} of the complete
relation but only of its bearing on one of its members.
According to Sir W.H. we speak of both members equally; but
this is not the case, we speak one of the other; we refer one to
the other and we represent it only in so far as we refer it to the
other. When I define; thus "A is the first letter" My subject is really
"A and the first letter" and "identical" is the predicate.

To this argument from universal grammar the Hamiltonians make
an explicit reply. This is that "ordinary language is mainly
concerned about what is thought -- not the manner of thinking
it." This, according to them is sufficient to account for it.

non quantification of the predicate, in ordinary language.
But I assert, and appeal to everyone, that all representation
including all thought, is concerned with what is represented or
thought and not with the manner of representing or thinking it.
Is not this obvious ? {del}As{/del} therefore in the case of ordinary language
it was claimed that this circumstance explained the non-quanti-
fication of the predicate ; I claim that it necessitates the non-
quantification of the predicate in all cases. This {add}implies invariably{/add} {del}has been urged{/del}
shows a confusion of ideas; logic it is true does not deal with the
matter of thought, but then it as certainly deals with thought as
having matter that is as being a representation - true or false.

Another reply made to the grammatical objection is that men are so
in the habit of dealing with thoughts that when I say “all men
are mortal” they at once assume that “all men are some mortals”
is what is intended. But tell a savage that “all men are immortal.”
Will he immediately begin to think whether some others are immortal
or not? No, his mind will not be directed to others at all; he
will be able to think that all men are immortal without con-
sidering that question one way or the other, that is he will think it
without quantifying the predicate. This reply, therefore, is far from
being satisfactory because if it were necessary to thought to quanti-
fy the predicate one way or the other, we should have to express
the quantification whenever we made any unusual statement.

part of the air is rainy.

If in the formula given for the minor indirect syllogism,
we put "false" for X, we obtain the following :

        M is P,
        That S is P is false,
        Hence, that S is M is false.

This mood is called Bocardo. Making the same substitution
in the major indirect syllogism, we get

        S is M,
        That S is P is false,
        Hence, that M is P is false.

This mood is called Baroko. Baroko and Bokardo are simple
cases of the reductio ad absurdum. Baroko is useful for
establishing distinctions between things, as when we reason,

        All fishes are propogated from eggs,
        But it is not true that whales are propogated from eggs,
        Therefore, it is not true that whales are fishes.

Bokardo is useful for establishing exceptions to rules, as
thus :
        The dingo is a mammal of Australia,
        But it is not true that dingos carry their young in
pouches,
        Therefore, it is not true that all the mammals of
Australia carry their young in pouches.

There are many other modes of syllogism derivable in similar

ways, but they are of no great importance, and will be        19

assed by for the moment.

FIFTY METHOD.  The principle of excluded
middle.

We have thus far considered but a single property
of negation, namely that denying a proposition is applying
a general to it. The formula of the modus ponens gives us
by transformation this form of immediate inference :

        S is true,
        Hence, if from S P follows, then P is true.

Substitute for P the special description "false" and we
have the immediate inference

        S is true,
        Hence, the denial of S is false.

The statement of the validity of this general inference
is termed the principle of contradiction. The converse of
this principle, namely that from the denial of the denial
of a proposition the truth of that proposition follows
is termed the principle of excluded middle. This principle
constitutes a distinct axiom concerning negation. It is not
involved in any of the traditional forms of inference,
but it is involved in certain modes introduced into logic
by De Morgan. I do not mean that reasoning involving this
principle had never been used before, but only that such

forms had escaped recognition as distinct from others, and
indeed De Morgan himself does not see that they are essen-
tially different from the rest. The inference from “If A
is true, B is false,” to “If B is true, A is false,” depends
merely on the principle of contradiction, that is on “false”
being a general predicate. For “false” put any predicate, C,
and it will be equally true that if it be admitted that
A being granted, C follows from B, we must also admit that
B being granted, C follows from A. On the other hand, the
inference from “If A is false, B is true,” to “If B is
false, A is true,” is of an entirely different nature,
and depends on the principle of excluded middle.

METHODS OF REASONING.                                      1

FIRST METHOD. {add}The simple consequence,{/add}

{del}[unclear: A proposition which might]
[unclear: rightly be believed for]
[illegible]{/del}

Reasoning is accepting something as true,
while recognizing some other proposition already accepted
as the reason for it. By a proposition, is meant, in logic,
anything which can be held for true, or can be supposed
to be so held. By recognizing a proposition as a reason
for another, is meant recognizing that the belief in the
former proposition causes a belief in the second, and ought
to do so, if we wish to adhere to the truth. The rudest
kind of reasoning consists in recognizing one proposition
as the reason for another, without recognizing any definite
relation between them which should make one the reason
for the other. A proposition accepted on account of a reason
is called a conclusionand is said to be inferred from that
reason, which is called the premise of the inference.
Let A and B denote two propositions : then such an inference
as we are speaking of may be written, "A, therefore B."
This inference is called a simple consequence. Its premise
or reason is called the antecedent : its conclusion the
consequent. The law or principle according to which one
proposition causes a belief in the other, say such a propo-
sition as "If A is true, B is true," is called, like the
process of inference, itself, the consequence. In a very
unreflecting state of mind, we may not recognize the con-
sequence, as a premise : although we must have some feeling
that there is such a consequence, for without this we should
not regard the one belief as causing the other. I hear someone
make some statement, and immediately jump to a belief in
what he says, without stopping to consider whether he is
a veracious witness or not. Again, I see a magician put
a pocket-handkerchief in a box, and am unreflectingly led
to believe it remains in the box, five minutes after, without
asking myself whether that is what generally happens in
the case of magicians' tricks, or not.

A simple consequence may really be sound (or as the
technical phrase is, valid) reasoning because the principle
by which one belief causes the other, though not recognized
as a premise, may really be of such a kind that it will
never lead from truth to error ; but it is, as the logicians
say, very incomplete reasoning, because there is no securi-
ty that it is sound, no sufficient reason is given to prevent
the possibility of error.* There is but one kind of simple
consequence which completely justifies itself,-- if it can
be called reasoning at all. It is an inference of the form,
"A, therefore A." To continue to believe a thing because
we have already accepted it (while always holding ourselves
ready to reexamine it, if there be any motive for doing
so) is perfectly logical proceeding not open to criticism.

* Another recognized mode of expressing this is to say that B
may follow from A, but does not follow logically; also that A may be
a reason of B but is not a logical reason.

            (y-v)B' = (z-v):B'

meaning that "If y is true, z is true," we can eliminate y

            (x-v)AA' = (z-v):BB'

which signifies that "If x is true, z is true."This form
of inference is essentially that which the logicians term
Barbara.
     When we say that a proposition cannot be both true
and false, we mean not in any one case, for in one case
it may, of course, be true and in another false. By one
case, here, we mean at any one time, of any one thing,
in any one sense, etc. Let a designate any case of a given
definite series of cases. It makes no difference whether
this series is a class of things of which the proposition
might be true, or a range of possibilities or what. We may
be considering the class of men with reference to their
possessing some character, or the cholera as being preva-
lent at some or all of certain times, or a social phenome-
non as occuring in a certain range of possible social con-
stitutions, o r anything of the sort. Whatever be the nature
of the series of cases, we may write x_a to mean that in the
case a, x is true. [unclear: T?]

Probability differs from an ordinary investigation in the fact
that in Probability I seek to ascertain the number of cases under the
generic description and the number of cases under the specific descrip-
tion and thus to obtain in figures the percentage of truth in the propo-
sition.

What is the probability of rain tomorrow? If I had a complete
description of tomorrow the description would {del}disclose{/del} {add}determine{/add} the fact and there
would be no Probability about it. But all I know about tomorrow is its
yesterday; to wit, to-day. My imperfect description of to-day is the
only description I have of tomorrow. I count the tomorrows that had yes-
terdays like to-day. I count how many {add}such tomorrows{/add} were rainy. These numerical fig-
ures determine the Probability based upon this evidence.

What is the nature of necessary reasoning?

{del}true{/del}
The answer {del}to this question{/del} is very different
from any that the logical treatises give; very
different too from any that the sensational
philosophers give. It appears to be {del}to be{/del}
afforded, beyond all rational dispute, by
the logic of relatives. To bring out this answer,
I propose to submit some instances of
necessary reasoning to analysis. I will
not, however, choose any of the examples that
the treatises afford; they are too difficult.
Nor that it is too difficult to infer their
conclusions from their premises; but that
the reasoning in them is so slight and

inconveniences that acute minds have been
unable to perceive that any reasoning lies
therein; and a powerful logical microscope
is required to detect in them the rudiments of
the features which are characteristic of
necessary reasoning. This is true even in
those cases in which the multitude of terms
and the repetition of some of them in three
or more premises serve to conceal the
conclusion a little.

The proper method of study is to commence
with pieces of reasoning which are strongly
marked with the characteristics of necessary
inference, especially with that of decidedly
advancing the comprehension of the subjects
to which they relate. It is in mathematics
chiefly in which such demonstrations are to
be found; in other subjects {del}there{/del} {add}demonstrations{/add} are either
ineffectual or fallacious or seem to be mere

applications of mathematics, or imitations
of the procedures of mathematics. Taking
then the demonstrations of a few mathe-
matical theorems, — not mere corollaries,
— one should analyze them with the utmost
minuteness, {del}care{/del} and accuracy, and note
carefully {del}their{/del} what their effectiveness is
due to. From these, one can pass on
to other reasonings too slight to be easy
of dissection.

This study is not a genial one. In fact,
it is so excessively dry and crabbed that I
have long hesitated to invite any readers
to follow me in it; yet the results are of
so great importance, and so unattainable
in any other way, that my scruples have
at length been overcome.

Each branch of mathematics is the

study of the relations involved in some
ideal system. I might say an ideal infinite
system; for finite systems, such as the icosi-
hedrons, form only subordinate subjects of
study. Of infinite systems there are two kinds,
the discrete, or number, and the continuous,
or {del}[unclear: place]{/del} space.

Of systems of number the chief is that
of the positive integers.

Mr Venn happily says that this theory is the great addition which  
the age of science has made to Logic, and that as the ancient  
Logic had to pass through a realistic and a conceptualistic phase  
before it reached the truth in nominalism, so the {del}[unclear: notion of class?]{/del}

by their probabilities), afterwards as belonging to the {del}conception{/del}  
belief in a fact as more or less vivid, finally as belonging only  
to the denominations under which events are collected together.

Lecture II

In the last lecture, the conception of logic was developed
from the point of view of scholastic realism or more pre-
cisely Thomism, and logic was defined as the science of the
relations of symbols in general to their Objects. The subject for
today is the theory of induction.

Hamilton and Mansell tell us that scientific induction
is extra logical. But there is certainly some logic in it, and
what it is needs pointing out; and these writers by throwing all that
is peculiar in it into the form of a premiss, virtually give up the
problem altogether. With this opinion, I must impose upon myself
the task of explaining all induction.

Aristotle begins his chapter of the subject {del}with{/del} this definition.
“Induction and the syllogism from induction is the syllogizing of
one extreme as a predicate to the middle, through the other extreme
This demands some explanation. Take this example

All carnivora are mammals
All mammals are vertebrates
∴ All carnivora are vertebrates

This is a common deductive syllogism. Of the three classes
spoken of are the terms. Of these, carnivora is the least extensive

Neat and Deer are herbivora
Neat & deer divide the hoof
Hence {del}animals{/del} dividing the hoof are herbivora


[unclear: 14048!]


All which divide the hoof are herbivora
Neat & deer    divide the hoof
∴ Neat & deer are herbivora

vertebrates is the most extensive and mammals is intermediate
{del}between{/del} the other two. The most and least extensive terms
are called the extremes, and by all later writers are distinguished
respectively as the major & minor extremes. The intermediate
term is called the middle. The syllogism consists in the synthesis
in the inferred proposition of the two terms not united in the
{del}[illegible]{/del} propositions which serve as data. The conclusion is the propo-
sition which follows apodictically from the other two which are
called the premises. Now in this example {del}of induction{/del}
        Such and such positions are {del}points{/del} on an ellipse
        The same positions are positions of Cebidae
Hence All positions of Cebidae are {del}points{/del} on an ellipse
is a true induction; characterized by {del}circumstances that{/del} {add}properly{/add} of inferring
a restriction of the whole from a restriction of the parts. But it is
not a syllogism, because the conclusion is not apodictic. The same
propositions may, however, be arranged so as to form a syllogism,
thus,      All positions of Cebidae are {del}points{/del} on an ellipse
        Such and such positions are positions of Cebidae
    ∴ Such and such positions are {del}points{/del} on an ellipse
The term, {del}on an ellipse{/del} {add}herbivora{/add}, is the major or most extensive extreme;
{add}animal with divided hoofs{/add}
{del}Cebidae{/del} is the middle term; and {del}Such and such positions{/del} {add}Neat & sheep{/add} is the mi-
nor extreme. It will be seen that in the major premiss we have
as {del}[illegible]{/del}totle says “syllogized the major premiss as predicate of

Synopsis of the Discussion of the Ground of Induction.     Ma. 766     1

I

The first and most obvious theory (when we approach the subject
from the side of probability) is Laplace's {add}[unclear: criterion?]{/add} that the probability of
an inductive conclusion can be calculated by inverse proba-
bilities.

To that I make two objections:
1st, that it is an idea founded on want of clear apprehension
of the nature of probability, which signifies security in the Long
run. Since there is but one universe of which we have experience
it is absurd to speak of the probability that that universe is subject
to a given law.
2nd, the calculation is founded upon {add}the assumption of{/add}
{del}[unclear]{/del} "equal possibilities"
This is basing a conclusion on ignorance, which is absurd.

Besides, even if we admit equal possibilities, {add}the equal{/add}
{del}[unclear]{/del}
possibilities, if rightly enumerated are not, for example, that
out of 8 days the tide should rise 0 days, 1 day, 2 days, 3 days
etc. But the equal possibilities, — if there be such a thing, — are {del}[unclear]{/del}
{del}the tide should not rise falling{/del} (designating a day on which the tide
rises by O and one on which it does not rise as X) as follows:
[diagram: three columns of O/X sequences separated by vertical lines, enumerating possible patterns of tide rising or not rising over days; final entry partly deleted; “etc.” below]

now upon that supposition, it will be found that the probability that the tides

about as nearly {del}[unclear]{/del} under mere circumstances as those [unclear: which]

will rise on the 8th day is just the same whatever it may have done on the
previous seven days.

{del}And{/del} To these objections the reply is made that I merely define pro-
bability. That it is not the ratio of frequency, but the measure of
the degree of confidence we ought to have in the occurrence of a con-
tingent event.

To that I rejoin,
1st, that a person can take a word in any sense he clearly defines;
but if this object is to do any useful thought, it is better to make it mean
something with reference to facts of a practical kind.
2nd, if it be said that we ought to have a certain degree of confi-
dence in an event, the question is on what principle we ought to
do so. The only principle I can recognize is that we ought to base our
conduct on such rules as will lead to the greatest practical advantage
in the long run. {del}If all we know is that{/del} Now the rule of greatest prac-
tical advantage in the long run is to make {add}the ratio{/add} our contingent winning to our
contingent losses {add}on each occasion{/add} as much greater as possible than the ratio of frequency
of losses to the frequency of gains. It therefore seems that ratios of frequency
are after all the only clear conception. Now {del}the ratio of frequency which
is equal to the probability{/del} Laplace assigns to inductive conclusions
{add}necessarily{/add} is equal to no other ratio of frequency than the frequency with
which among a vast number of universes we should meet with
a universe constituted in a certain way provided we know that
in general different constitutions of universes would be met with
with equal frequency. The ratio of frequency has no application to any real

I shall urge a very sweeping and strange objection. All
these definitions regard logic as referring exclusively to thought
or cognition. Yet thoughts and cognitions are events which
happen in a particular subject at particular times; whereas logical
relevance has no such limits and remains whether it is thought
or not. Two replies will be made to this objection; first, that
by thought is meant thought in general {del}and that{/del} in which the limi-
tations of the particular are eliminated; and second that lo-
gical relations only exist by being thought and that therefore
it is not true that they remain whether thought or not. To the first
part of this reply; I rejoin,

§1. Conception of Logic.

Against the common conception of logic, I shall urge a
sweeping objection. {del}Logic{/del} Almost every definition of the science
that has ever been propounded has made it refer exclusively to
thought or cognition. Now, if by thought is {del}it{/del} meant thought as
it is, I object that thought is concrete and limited in time and
otherwise while the relations of logic have no such limitation.
Suppose a syllogism to be written down; then every time that it
is read it produces {del}cognition{/del}{add}conviction{/add}; and this shows that it has a
permanent convincing power or relevancy in itself, as much as
the repeatedly observed blackness of the letters shows that they have
a quality of blackness in themselves. But if by thought be
meant thought in general from which everything that is particular
has been eliminated, I then reply that such thought cannot really
be thought; it is too pure and abstract; and that consequently, logic
does not deal with psychological laws as is now commonly sup-
posed. In fact, thought may be illogical; it is only correct
thought which is logical. What is this correct thought? It is thought
which represents the intuition. Logic therefore deals with thought
only in so far as the latter is a representation. And as I said
every representation has its logical relations whether it is actually
thought or not. So that it is more correct to say that Logic is

the science of the forms of representation than that it is the
science of the forms of thought.
It is clear, however, that not all representations are subjects
of logic. There are three kinds of representation; which I denominate
                              Signification
                              Imitation
                              Verity
A sign is a representation which accords with its object without any
real and essential correspondence.
A copy is a representation which really and in its self refers to
its object by resembling it.
A {del}type{/del}{add}symbol{/add} is a representation whose correspondence with its object
is of {del}that{/del}{add}same{/add} immaterial kind as a sign but is founded nevertheless in
its very nature and {add}is{/add} {add}not{/add} merely supposed and fictitious.

Essays on the Rationale of Science
By C. S. Peirce.
Preface.

My purpose in this preface is to set forth the general pur-
pose of these essays; and particularly that of the series
comprising the first five, which are a revision of six
articles printed in the Popular Science Monthly in November,
1877 and January, March, April, June, and August, 1878. It
happened to me in the former year to find myself a
{del}[illegible]{/del} {add}[unclear: ship-mate?]{/add} in a ten days passage across the Atlantic
of the celebrated publisher Mr. William H. Appleton. The
pleasantly engaged of those days gave one time to make
acquaintances, even friendships. Mr. Appleton and I used
to pace the deck together, and I would talk to him about

Dear Signor C{add}a{/add}olderoni:
        I have delayed thanking you, as I now very warmly do,
for sending me the three numbers of Leonardo,{add}3{/add} and for your too
flattering references to my formulation.  In the April number
of the Monist{add}3{/add} I proposed that the word "pragmatism" should
hereafter be used somewhat loosely to signify affiliation with
Schiller, James, Dewey, Royce, and the rest of us, while the
particular doctrine which I invented the word to denote, which
is your first kind of pragmatism, should be called "pragmaticism."
The extra syllable will indicate the narrower meaning.
        Pragmaticism is not a system of philosophy.  It is only
a method of thinking; and your correspondent, Juliano il Sofista,
is quite right in saying that it is not a new way of thinking.
If it were so, that, to my mind, would be almost sufficient to
condemn it.  It is only the formulation of it which was new thirty
years ago, unless your correspondent is prepared to cite the
volume and page on which an equivalent formulation had already
been given.  From his tone, I infer that he is quite prepared
to do this; and I shall thus congratulate myself on an unknown
fellow-thinker.  Of those who have used this way of thinking
Berkeley is the clearest example, though Locke (especially in
the fourth book of his Essay), Spinoza, and Kant may be claimed
as adherents of it.
        Although pragmaticism is not a philosophy, yet, as you
rightly say, it best comports with the English philosophy, and
more particularly with the Scotch doctrine of common sense.

2

[unclear: 6 word to?]

In an article which should have appeared in the July
Monist but which seems to have been crowded out by matters of
superior importance, magic squares and the like, I specify six
errors which I find in the Scotch doctrine of common sense, of
which the most important is that those philosophers failed to
remark the extreme vagueness of our indubitable beliefs.  For
example, everybody's actions show that it is impossible to doubt
that there is an element of order in the world; but the moment
we attempt to define that orderliness we find room for doubt.
There is, besides, another respect in which pragmaticism is at
issue not only with English philosophy more particularly, but
with all modern philosophy more or less, even with {add}H{/add}Hegel; and
that is that it involves a complete rupture with nominalism.
Even Dun{add}s{/add} Scotus is too nominalistic when he says that universals
are contracted to the mode of individuality in singulars, meaning,
as he does, by singulars, ordinary existing things.  The pragma-
ticist cannot admit that.  I myself went too far in the direction
of nominalism when I said that it was a mere question of the con-
venience of speech whether we say that a diamond is hard when it
is not pressed upon, or whether we say that it is soft until it
is pressed upon.  I now say that experiment will prove that the
diamond is hard, as a positive fact.  That is, it is a real fact
that it would resist pressure, which amounts to extreme scholastic
realism.  I deny that pragmaticism as originally defined by me
made the intellectual purport of symbols to consist in our conduct.
On the contrary, I was most careful to say that it consists in
our concept of what our conduct would be upon conceivable occasions.

For I had long before declared that absolute individuals were
entia rationis, and not realities.  A concept determinate in all
respects is as fictitious as a concept definite in all respects.
I do not think we can ever have a logical right to infer, even,
{add}ly is ed.{/add}
as probable, the existence of anything entire{add}ly{/add} contrary in its
nature to all that we can experience or imagine.  But a nominalist
must do this.  For he must say that all future events are the total
of all that will have happened and therefore that the future is
not endless; and therefore, that there will be an event not
followed by any event.  This may be, inconceivable as it is; but
the nominalist must say that it will be, else he will make the
future to be endless, that is, to have a mode of being consisting
in the truth of a general law.  For every future event will have
been completed, but the endless future will not have been completed.
There are many other turns that may be given to this argument; and
the conclusion of it is that it is only the general which we can
understand.  What we commonly designate by pointing at it or
otherwise indicating it we assume to be singular.  But so far as
we can comprehend it, it will be found not to be so.  We can only
indicate the real universe; if we are asked to describe it, we
can only say that it includes whatever there may be that really is.
This is a universal, not a singular.

        The truth of pragmaticism may be proved in various ways.
I would conduct the argument somewhat as follows.  In the first
{add}there are but three elementary kinds of reasoning: The first,{/add}
place, which I call abduction (on the theory, the doubtful theory,
I confess, that the meaning of the XXVth chapter of the second
book of the Prior Analytics has been completely diverted from

4

Aristotle's meaning by a single wrong word having been inserted
by Appellicon where the original word was illegible) consists in
examining a mass of facts and in allowing these facts to suggest
a theory.  In this way we gain new ideas; but there is no force
in the reasoning.  The second kind of reasoning is deduction, or
necessary reasoning.  It is applicable only to an ideal state of
things, or to a state of things in so far as it may conform to
an ideal.  It merely gives a new aspect to the premisses.  It
consists in constructing an image or diagram in accordance with
a general precept, in observing in that image certain relations
of parts not explicitly laid down in the precept, and in convincing
oneself that the same relations will always occur when that precept
is followed out.  For example, having convinced ourselves of the
truth of the p{add}o{/add}ns asinorum with the aid of a diagram drawn with
a common lead pencil, we are quite sure it would be the same
with a diagram drawn in red; and a form of syllogism which is
certain in black is equally so in red.  A phenomenon having been
observed in a laboratory, though we may not know on what conditions
it depends, yet we are quite sure that it would make no difference
whether the number of degrees of the longitude of the planet
Eros just one week previous were a prime or composite number.
The third way of reasoning is induction, or experimental research.
Its procedure is this. Abduction having suggested a theory, we
employ deduction to deduce from that ideal theory a promiscuous
variety of consequences to the effect that if we perform certain
acts, we shall find ourselves confronted with certain experiences.
We then proceed to try these experiments, and if the predictions

5

of the theory are verified, we have a proportionate confidence
that the experiments that remain to be tried will confirm the
theory.  I say that these three are the only elementary modes
of reasoning there are.  I am conviced of it both a priori and
a posteriori.  The a priori reasoning is contained in my paper
in the Proceedings of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences
for April 9, 1867.  I will not repeat it.  But I will mention that
it turns in part upon the fact that induction is, as Aristotle
says, the inference of the truth of the major premiss of a syllogism
of which the minor premiss is made to be true and the conclusion
is found to be true, while abduction is the inference of the truth
of the minor premiss of a syllogism of which the major premiss is
selected as known already to be true while the conclusion is found
to be true.  Abduction furnishes all our ideas concerning real
things, beyond what are given in perception, but is mere conjecture,
without probative force.  Deduction is certain but relates only to
ideal objects.  Induction gives us the only approach to certainty
concerning the real that we can have.  In forty years diligent
study of arguments, I have never found one which did not consist
of these elements.  The successes of modern science ought to con-
vince us that induction is the only capable imperator of truth-
seeking.  Now.pragmaticism is simply the doctrine that the
inductive method is the only essential to the ascertainment of the
intellectual purport of any symbol.

This argument must be supplemented by examples of the
wholesome effect of pragmatistic interpretations.  Among the most

{add}Conclusion{/add}

signal of these is the explanation of probability. We begin
by asking, what is the use of calculations of probabilities;
and the answer is that the great business of insurance rests
upon such calculations. The probability upon which this business
proceeds consists in the practical certainty that for every ten
thousand dollars paid in about a certain number of dollars will
have to be paid out. In the rare,-- the very rare, case in which
decidedly more must be paid out, there are not only reserves more
than ample, but there is the knowledge that such large payments
will cause a great increase in the amounts paid in. A probability,
therefore, is the known ratio of frequency of a specific future
event to a generic future event which includes it. That is what
probability must mean in order to have any importance for business.
What, then, does it mean to say that if a man sees a phenomenon

even by sleep, I produced my one contribution to philosophy in
the "New List of Categories" in the Proceedings of the American

my maxim of pragmatism; and that I take pride in the entire absence
