§12 Division of Formal Science.                                      Ms. 809

Every {del}[unclear: re alio]{/del} reference which is imprescindible from another is sub-
ject to certain conditions in consequence, because conceptions are
not combined without some form of combination. But a concep-
tion which is prescindible is {add}unconditional or{/add} independent absolute, and is subject to no
This will be illustrated presently.

Now the imprescindible references are

1  The reference of the equiparavants to their correlate
2  The reference of the disquiparand to its ground
3  The reference of the disquiparand to its correlate
4  The reference of the likeness to its interpretant
5  The reference of the index to its object
6  The reference of the index to its interpretant
7  The reference of the symbol to its ground,
8  The reference of the symbol to its object,
9  The reference of the symbol to its interpretant.

The conditions of these nine references afford nine formal sciences,
{del}not{/del} by which {del}are{/del} we {add}may{/add} indicate very extensive doctrines but
one which forms a system by itself. Let us briefly, [unclear: Then] [unclear: possible?]
Equiparavants refer to each of these must now be briefly illustrated.
{add}before this initial{/add}

.Reference of equiparavants to their correlates. The ground of equiparavanc ad-
mits of quantity; it is greater or less, according as it is more or less
determinate. This quantity is termed the comprehension intension or
depth of the equiparance. Thus, birds and lobsters agree in being animals

& “birds and fishes agree in being vertebrate animals;” But
the latter equivarance is more {add}in ten{/add} comprehensive that the former.
The object of equivarance admits of quantity also; being greater
the greater number of individuals are comprehended under it.
This quantity is the extension or breadth of the equivarance.
{del}These{/del} Those things which agree in being animals are more nu-
merous than those which agree in being vertebrate animals.

-A-
{del}Equivarance{/del} the relate & {add}the{/add} correlate of equivarance, is related
to its relate {del}by equi{/del} {add}upon{/add} the same ground & in the same manner as
the relate to the correlate. Accordingly, in equivarance there
is no distinction of relate and correlate; for any distinction
would at once constitute a disequivarance. The {del}mere{/del} {add}conception{/add} of
a correlate {del}includes{/del} {add}in its narrower sense{/add}, does not therefore belong to equi-
varance and it {add}perhaps{/add} is preferable to speak of the reference of {add}the relates{/add} equivarants
{del}among{/del} each other, than of “the reference of equivarants to their correlates”
Equivarance is in fact the constitution of a class, and a correlate of
an equivarance is nothing more than another member of the
same class. By a class is meant a number of agreeing things.
(now take the first ¶, and then)

In general, the greater the extension of a class the less its intension
and vice versa. {del}For{/del}; if we add to all the individuals which agree
in a certain respect another individual, it is clear that the new sum
of individuals do not agree in so many respects as the old; and if we
substitute for {add}one{/add} a class another where members agree in {add}the same respects besides{/add} more respects,
it is clear that the former class must include the latter & another class
besides.

215

This {del}therefore{/del} of the reciprocal ratio* of extension & intension
formed by itself a science of classes or correlates of the reference
of equiforms to their correlates. It may be called —

2 Reference of the disquiparant to its ground. The ground of a disquipar-
ance has an intimate connection with a ground of equiparance.
Thus blues is connected with blue. This connection is such, that the
ground of disquiparance includes the ground of equiparance and has
besides a special determination which the latter has not. This is not
evident in {add}the case{/add} of a disquiparance which is merely an {add}equiparance{/add}
undetermined as to its ground as when we say the elephant is as strong
as the hippopotamus, which, is merely as one {add}considered in relation{/add} of the elephant &
the hippopotamus {del}based on{/del} a certain degree of strength. But the ground
of equiparance implied in this disquiparance is one less determinate than
that of this equiparance “is stronger a hippopotamus;” for “the elephant is
{add}as strong as the{/add}
hippopotamus” implies that the elephant and the hippopotamus agree
in hippopotomus like strength.” But the disquiparance {add}implies{/add} that they
are also diverse and therefore have some further determination be-
sides that {add}in which they agree{/add}. {del}in the equiparance{/del}

The correlate of a disquiparant has a relation, {add}α disquiparance{/add}, to its relate, {add}upon a ground which may be different from{/add}
the relation of the relate to the correlate. But these two grounds determine
each other. Thus if A exceeds B, B is exceeded by A. This mutual de-
termination of the two grounds is expressed by saying that one is the inverse
of the other. {del}A ground of disquiparance considered as establishing a direct{/del}
disquiparance is called active; a disquiparance when the inverse ground

is called passive. Thus A is greater than B is an active disqui-
parance; A is {del}active{/del} {add}that{/add} which B is less; is a passive disqui-
parance. Sometimes the inverse grounds are the same; thus, that
A equals B is the same as that A is equaled by B. The inverse of
the inverse of a ground is always the ground itself; thus that "A is
{add}something by which{/add} B is exceeded" is the same as that A exceeds B.
{add}[This is genuine]{/add}
If B is disquiparant to C, upon the same ground on which A is disqui-
parant to B, then A is disquiparant to C upon the same ground also.
But if the grounds of the two first disquiparances are different
this does not follow. If the ground of the disquiparance of B to C is the
inverse of that of A to B, then the disquiparance of A to C {add}has{/add} is either
one ground or the other or else rests upon some different ground altogether
{add}does not exist{/add}
or {del}upon none{/del} at all; and {add}in any case{/add} there is no ground of dis-
quiparance between A & B which there was not between A & B or B & C.
This {del}fact enables acquaintance with{/del} {add}relation gives rise to{/add} a peculiar quantity of grounds
of disquiparance; {del}for the depending upon the number of repetitions{/del}
of a given inverse {add}disquiparance relation{/add} which shall first neutralize a dis-
quiparance. Thus, let the {add}ground{/add} disquiparance of A to B be the in-
verse of that of B to C and {del}the ground{/del} {add}suppose{/add} that they are such that there is
no disquiparance between A & C. Now suppose that the ground of the
disquiparance of B to C be simply inverted, that is suppose it to change
from active to passive without altering in any other respect whatever. Then
we have a disquiparance between A & C which is just twice that be-
tween A & B. {add}And thus we introduce the conception of measure. No other kind
of quantity, except that of the ground of a disquiparance, can be measured.{/add}
The science of the reference of disquiparants to their grounds; that is the
science of measurable quantity, is called mathematics. When only in-
versions are taken into account, it is algebra. When differences in the kind of
ground is taken into account, it affords (for example) geometry.

31}

[diagram: pen trials, zigzag and wavy strokes]

3 Reference of the disquiparant to its correlate. {del}The correlate of a disqui-
parant{/del} is said to be the term, exclusion in extension and opposition,
indicate the reference of a disquiparant to its correlate. The ground
of a correlate besides that quantity which is measureable, and, which
{add}and which may be termed its immediate quantity,{/add}
we {del}has{/del} have just treated, has another also which is similar to the {add}quantity of the{/add} ground
of equiparance, which may be called its intension comprehension or {del}[unclear: relation]{/del}
it is the quantity of the ground considered as {del}embodied in{/del} {add}embodied inversely in{/add} the correlates, [unclear: one]
may be termed the mediate quantity. This quantity is the number of respects
in which there is = disquiparance. The number of individual & disquiparant
correlates may be termed the extension of the disquiparance. The extension
& intension of disquiparance are in inverse ratio to each other. But
there is this remarkable difference between the law of equiparance &
that of disquiparance is, {add}namely,{/add} that equiparance {del}without{/del} {add}entirely undetermined in{/add} {add}no relation{/add}
at all; {del}while{/del} {add}and{/add} equiparance of {del}complete by{/del} the total intension is {del}as we{/del} {add}relation{/add}
that of identity; whereas disquiparance of entirely undetermined intension
is a most important relation, that of contradiction as between man
& non-man; {del}while{/del} {add}and{/add} disquiparance of the whole intension is impos-
sible. These remarks are sufficient to show that the science of oppo-
sition usually made a part of logic, is the science of the reference
of disquiparants to their correlates.

4 Reference of the likeness to its interpretant.

[illegible]

[unclear: A mental representation] is something which puts the
mind into relation to an object. A representation
generally (I am here defining my use of the term)
is something which brings one thing into relation with
another. The conception of third is here involved,
and therefore, also, the conceptions of second or other
and of first or an. A representation is in fact
nothing but a something which has a third through an
other. We may therefore consider an object:

1. as a something, with inward determinations;

2. as related to an other;

3. as bringing a second into relation to a third.

[illegible]
[illegible]
[illegible]
[illegible]
[illegible]
[unclear: of] [illegible] [unclear: psychological] [illegible]

of a psychological character. We can therefore reach no {del}prop{/del}
{del}dubious{/del} conclusions which are necessarily limited to
{del}the human mind{/del} concerning human thought which be may
not extend to any representation, in the mind or out of it.
Every system of signs has certain properties. In virtue of
these properties certain propositions hold good in that sys-
tem of signs, {del}without ref{/del} independently of the existence
of the thing signified. For example, it is true that a grif-
fin is a winged quadruped; but there is no fact which
corresponds to this proposition and makes it true, except
the fact that the word griffin is so used. It is a familiar
fact that the earliest {del}mode{/del} way of using signs is to think
in them without thinking of them, as signs; that is to say, the
thought of the sign does suggest another thought, but it is not
distinctly thought of as suggesting {add}the other{/add} another thought. In this
simple thought, — the first intention, — propositions whose
validity depends on the properties of the system of signs employed,
cannot be regarded {del}in their true nature{/del} as merely verbal,
but appear to be {del}propositions concerning things.{/del} {add}expressed as facts.{/add} And even
after reflection has shown the true nature of {add}such{/add} {del}these{/del} pro-
positions, this reflection though more or less intimately as-
sociated with the thought of the sign, yet remains a second
thought, distinct from the first, and so more or less of
the old illusion {add}lingers{/add} {del}remains{/del}, even after it is known that it

to an illusion. If there are any properties which belong to
representations, in general, certain propositions will hold
good in consequence in all thoughts, and these propo-
sitions will always appear to be {del}propositions{/del} {add}statements{/add} of fact
although they really involve no fact except the gener-
al {del}laws{/del} ones concerning signs. It is plainly very important
to ascertain whether there are any such propositions and
what they are, for the necessity of these is entirely dif-
ferent from that of general facts, since these do not de-
pend on the character of the things signified but only on
the {del}characters{/del} {add}properties{/add} of the signs as signifying them. {add}were we{/add} the line of demar-
cation between the two kinds of necessity is all the deeper
if the proposition {del}that{/del} all signs have certain properties derives
its validity from the properties of the special system of signs in
which we always think.

Now in point of fact from the general conception of
a representation, certain things may be deduced. These deduc-
tions are of a sort which can only be {del}performed{/del} {add}made{/add} by general sup-
positions of individual cases. They, therefore, present the pecul
{add}The fact that these{/add}
{del}Then conclusions therefore are not being derivable with{/del} {add}are{/add} own The re-
alization of a supposition not involved in the premises, in their
pure generality, gives them a certain resemblance to induction.
But as this supposition is essentially general, its general
character being determined by the premise and its special

character being of no consequence, they are in fact deductions.
A mental representation is something which puts the
mind into relation to an object. A representation generally,
({add}any defining [unclear: more?]{/add} [unclear: in that] use of the term) is something which brings one thing into
relation with another. The conception of third is here involved,
and therefore, also, the conceptions of second or other + and of
first or one. A representation is in fact nothing but a
{del}third something with one second and a third{/del} something
which has a third through an other. We may therefore consider
an object
        1. as a something, with inward determinations;
        2. as related to an other;
        3. as bringing a second into relation to a third.

Ma. 810

Conclusion

It may be well to conclude with a brief recapitulation
of the fundamental principles of the Logic of relatives.
{del}All terms{/del}, then, are either absolute or relative. Relative
terms are either simple relatives or conjugatives. Conjuga-
tives are reducible to relatives of two correlates. The
most important division of simple relatives is into con-
currents and opponents. The former imply an agreement
in some respect among objects, the latter set {del}one{/del} {add}the relate{/add} over
against its correlate. "Men that is --" is a concurrent, "hus-
band of -" is an opponent. The division of conjugatives
needs further investigation.

Every simple relative has its converse, so that simple rela-
tives generally occur in pairs; as "lover of -" and "loved by -"
Conjugatives of two correlates occur in sixes; as "given of -
to -," "given to - of -," "presented by - with -," "presented with
- by -," "given to - by -," "given by - to -."

{del}A correlative may either be undistributed, (as in "love of
a woman") or distributed (as in "love of every woman"). If
distributed it is so positively (as in "love of every woman")
or negatively (as in "love of nothing but a woman."){/del}

A correlate may be attached to a relative in three
ways; first without distribution as in "love of a woman";
second with distribution of correlate, as in "love of every woman,"

then, with distribution of relate as in "lover," in whatever way
it is lover at all, of a woman."

Relatives may be compounded. In their composition, {add}four{/add} {del}certain{/del} prin-
ciples hold, which may best be enuciated in examples. Sup-
pose lover and servant to be compounded in their application
to woman. Then, we have,

1. That which stands to a woman in the compound relation of
lover of a servant is {del}the{/del} same as {add}the{/add} Lover of {add}[unclear: some] everything which is{/add} servant of a
woman; (De Morgan)

2. That which stands to every woman in the relation of Lover
of every servant is the same as the Lover of everything which
is servant of a woman;

[diagram: left margin brace grouping the next two numbered propositions, marked "h."]

3. That what stands to nothing but a woman in the relation of
lover of a servant is the same as the lover of nothing but
that which is servant of nothing but a woman;

4. That which stands to every woman in the relation of lover
of nothing but a servant is the same as that which is a lover
of nothing but what is a servant of every woman.

The converse of a compound relation is obtained by con-
verting the constituents and transposing their order, the dis-
tribution of the constituents remaining unchanged. (De Morgan)
Thus, the converse of {del}the{/del} "lover of every servant" is "master of
nothing but what is loved by --."

{del}Here{/del} the relation expressed by the copula is the most general,

consequence. This relation is transitive, (that is what is what is
X̄ is X̄) and continuous (that is {del}of{/del} X̄ is what is X̄.)
Connected closely with the simple relative “what is _” are two
conjugatives, “what is both _ and _” and “what is either _ or _,”
{add}two{/add} {add}(which I denote by (,) and +;){/add}
The laws of these relatives, present a perfect parallelism.
{del}They are as follows{/del}:—

[diagram: long vertical bracketing lines and curved arrows connecting paired laws]

1.  X̄ is either X̄ or Ȳ.
(What is both X and Y is X.)

2.  What is either X or X is X.
{del}What{/del} X is both X and X.

3.  What is either what is X̄ or Ȳ or else Z {add}is the same as what is{/add} either X or else what is ei-
ther Y or Z. What is both what is both X and Ȳ and also what
is Z is the same as what is both X and is also what is is [unclear: too] Ȳ
and Z.

4.  What is either X or Y is the same as what is either Y or X. What
is both X and Y is the same as what is both Y and X.

5.  What is both X and {add}either{/add} Y or Z is the same as what is {add}either{/add} both X and Y
or both X and Z. What is either X or both Y and Z is the same as
{del}what is both{/del} either X or Y and either X or Z.
(22) omit (23),

Compare (21) with (94), (2) with (8), (3) with (9), (7) with (25),
(12) with (132), (129) with (131), (11) with (128), (127) with (13).
From these two conjugatives, are derived the two absolute
terms being and nothing. Being is such that X is both X and a
being. Nothing is such that what is either X or nothing is X.

The laws of being and nothing are also generalized. Compare (34)
with (47), (38) with (35), (36) with (38), (37) with (42), (40) with
(46), (34) with (45), (39) with (48), (133) with (137).
From these conceptions we also derive the relative “not.” With
its fundamental principles, {del}It had{/del} what is both X and not X is
nothing, and Everything is either X or not X.

∴  M is P.

This is called perfect induction.  It would be better to call it formal
induction.
In a similar way, from the syllogism,

Any M is Π′ P′,

Any S is M;

∴ Any S is Π′ P′;

where Π′ P′ denotes the conjunction of all the characters of M, if
the conclusion and first premise are true, the second premise is true by
definition; so that we have the demonstrative form of argument,

Any M is Π′ P′,

Any S is Π′ P′;

∴ Any S is M.

This is reasoning from definition, or, as it may be termed, formal
hypothesis.
One half of all possible propositions are true, because every prop-
osition has its contradictory.  Moreover, for every true particular
proposition there is a true universal proposition, and for every true
negative proposition there is a true affirmative proposition.  This
follows from the fact that the universal affirmative is the type of all
propositions.* Hence of all possible propositions in either of the forms,

Σ′ S′ is M, and M is Π′ P′,

one half are true.  In an untrue proposition of either of these forms,
some finite ratio of the S’s or P’s are not true subjects or predicates.
Hence, of all propositions of either of these forms which are partly
true, {del}some finite ratio more{/del} {add}a ratio greater by a finite amount{/add} than one half are wholly true.  Hence, if
in the above formula for formal induction or hypothesis, we substitute

* 1893. This seems to be an illicit introduction of
an ontological proposition {del}of a very important{/del} of
great importance, which I now deny. But I have
successively improved upon the theory of the validity
of induction and hypothesis in {del}general{/del} {add}Essays{/add}, VI, XI, XII, XVII.

§ 7. Mathematical Syllogisms.

A kind of argument very common in mathematics may be exem-
plified as follows:—

Every part is less than that of which it is a part,
Boston is a part of the Universe;
∴ Boston is less than the Universe.

This may be reduced to syllogistic form thus:—

Any relation of part to whole is a relation of less to greater,
The relation of Boston to the Universe is a relation of part to whole;
∴ The relation of Boston to the Universe is a relation of less to greater.

If logic is to take account of the peculiarities of such syllogisms,
it would be necessary to consider some propositions as having three
terms, subject, predicate, and object; and such propositions would be
divided into active and passive. The varieties in them would be
endless.

PART III. § 1. Induction and Hypothesis.

In the syllogism,

Any M is P,

Σ′ S′ is M;

∴ Σ′ S′ is P;

where Σ′ S′ denotes the sum of all the classes which come under M.
if the second premise and conclusion are known to be true, the first

* Hypotheticals have not been considered above, the well-known opinion having
been adopted that, “If A, then B,” means the same as “Every state of things in
which A is true is a state of things in which B is (or will be) true.”

—VOL. VII.          36

OF ARTS AND SCIENCES: APRIL 9, 1867.        283

S′ for Σ′ S′ and P′ for Π′ P′ we obtain formulæ of probable in-
ference.  This reasoning gives no determinate probability to these
modes of inference, but it is necessary to consider that, however
weak synthetic inference might have been at first, yet if it had
the least positive tendency to produce truth, it would continually
become stronger, owing to the establishment of more and more
secure premises.
  The rules for valid induction and hypothesis deducible from this
theory are as follows:—
  1. The explaining syllogism, that is to say, the deductive syllogism
one of whose premises is inductively or hypothetically inferred from
the other and from its conclusion, must be valid.
  2. The conclusion is not to be held as absolutely true, but only
until it can be shown that, in the case of induction, S′ was taken from
some narrower class than M, or, in the case of hypothesis, that P′ was
taken from some higher class than M.
  3. From the last rule it follows as a corollary that in the case of
induction the subject of the premises must be a sum of subjects, and
that in the case of hypothesis the predicate of the premises must be a
conjunction of predicates.
  4. Also, that this aggregate must be of different objects or qualities
and not of mere names.
  5. Also, that the only principle upon which the instanced subjects
or predicates can be selected is that of belonging to M.*

* Positivism, apart from its theory of history and of the relations between the
sciences, is distinguished from other doctrines by the manner in which it regards
hypotheses.  Almost all men think that metaphysical theories are valueless, be-
cause metaphysicians differ so much among themselves; but the positivists give
another reason, namely, that these theories violate the sole condition of all legiti-
mate hypothesis.  This condition is that every good hypothesis must be such as
is certainly capable of subsequent verification with the degree of certainty proper
to the conclusions of the branch of science to which it belongs.  There is, it seems
to me, a confusion here between the probability of a hypothesis in itself, and its
admissibility into any one of those bodies of doctrine which have received distinct
names, or have been admitted into a scheme of the sciences, and which admit only
conclusions which have a very high probability indeed.  I have here to deal with
the rule only so far as it is a general canon of the legitimacy of hypotheses, and not
so far as it determines their relevancy to a particular science; and I shall, therefore,
consider only another common statement of it; namely, “that no hypothesis is
admissible which is not capable of verification by direct observation.”  The positivist
regards all hypothesis, not as an inference, but as a device for stimulating and

-284          PROCEEDINGS OF THE AMERICAN ACADEMY

Hence the formulæ are
                         Induction.
        S' S'' S''', &c. are taken at random as M's,
             S' S'' S''', &c. are P ;
        ∴ Any M is probably P.

directing observation.  But I have shown above that certain premises will render an
hypothesis probable, so that there is such a thing as legitimate hypothetic inference.
It may be replied that such conclusions are not hypotheses, but inductions.  That the
sense in which I have used “hypothesis” is supported by good usage, I could prove by
a hundred authorities.  The following is from Kant: “An hypothesis is the holding
for true of the judgment of the truth of a reason on account of the sufficiency of its
consequents.”  Mill’s definition (Logic, Book III. Ch. XIV. § 4) also nearly coin-
cides with mine.  Moreover, an hypothesis in every sense is an inference, because it is
adopted for some reason, good or bad, and that reason, in being regarded as such,
is regarded as lending the hypothesis some plausibility.  The arguments which I
term hypothetic are certainly not inductions, for induction is reasoning from par-
ticulars to generals, and this does not take place in these cases.  The positivist
canon for hypotheses is neither sufficient nor necessary.  If it is granted that hy-
potheses are inferred, it will hardly be questioned that the observed facts must
follow apodictically from the hypothesis without the aid of subsidiary hypotheses,
and that the characters of that which is predicated in the hypothesis, and from which
the inference is drawn, must be taken as they occur, and not be picked out in order
to make a plausible argument.  That the maxim of the positivists is superfluous
or worse, is shown, first, by the fact that it is not implied in the proof that hypothetic
inference is valid; and next, by the absurdities to which it gives rise when strictly
applied to history, which is entirely hypothetical, and is absolutely incapable of
verification by direct observation.  To this last argument I know of but two
answers: first, that this pushes the rule further than was intended, it being con-
sidered that history has already been so verified; and second, that the positivist
does not pretend to know the world as it absolutely exists, but only the world which
appears to him.  To the first answer, the rejoinder is that a rule must be pushed
to its logical consequences in all cases, until it can be shown that some of these cases
differ in some material respect from the others.  To the second answer, the re-
joinder is double: first, that I mean no more by “is” than the positivist by
“appears” in the sense in which he uses it in saying that only what “appears” is
known, so that the answer is irrelevant; second, that positivists, like the rest of
the world, reject historic testimony sometimes, and in doing so distinguish hy-
pothetically between what is and what in some other sense appears, and yet have
no means of verifying the distinction by direct observation.

Another error in reference to hypothesis is, that the antecedent probability of what
is testified to cannot affect the probability of the testimony of a good witness.  This
is as much as to say that probable arguments can neither support nor weaken one
another.  Mr. Venn goes so far as to maintain the impossibility of a conflict of
probabilities.  The difficulty is instantly removed by admitting indeterminate
probabilities.

S is P' P'' P''', &c.;
∴ S is probably M.


§ 2. Moods and Figures of Probable Inference. {add}*{/add}

It is obvious that the explaining syllogism of an induction or
hypothesis may be of any mood or figure.
It would also seem that the conclusion of an induction or hypothesis
may be contraposed with one of the premises.


§ 3. Analogy.

The formula of analogy is as follows :—

S', S'', and S''' are taken at random from such
a class that their characters at random are such as P', P'', P'''.
t is P', P'', and P'''.
S', S'', and S''' are q.
∴ t is q.

Such an argument is double.  It combines the two following :—

1.

S', S'', S''' are taken as being P', P'', P'''.
S', S'', S''' are q.
∴ (By induction,) P', P'', P''' is q.
t is P', P'', P'''.
∴ (Deductively,) t is q.

2.

S', S'', S''' are, for instance, P', P'', P'''.
t is P', P'', P'''.
∴ (By hypothesis,) t has the common characters of S', S'', S'''.
S', S'', S''' are q.
∴ (Deductively,) t is q.

Voltaire laughed at Maupertuis offering to
prove the existence of a God by [formula: a^b over z?]; and
his foolish shaft affects to this day the reputation of
that profound philosopher. One of our newspapers
whose function is to maintain certain economical
{del}theses,{/del} to a refutation I offered of one of them
replied that my reasoning was “too much like the
differential calculus.” It was certainly like the
differential calculus, being substantially the same,
as Ricardo’s reasoning about rent; nor was
the “too much” altogether without its truth.
The same journal only the other day superciliously
condemned the use of mathematical language in
the expression of economical principles. The rea-
son given was that political economy is of no use
until the majority of the people embrace it. Its
straight must be desperate then; and no wonder a
language that leaves no openings for the shifts of ambi-
guity is eschewed. Professors of philosophy edu-

cated in theological seminaries are apt to fasten
something sinister lurking beneath any mathemati-
cal treatment of logic, and down to this day continue
{del}to censure{/del} such treatment magisterially. The very last
mail brought me the programme of a university
where Sir William Hamilton’s diatribe against the
study of mathematics is {del}made{/del} still seriously dis-
cussed. Every mathematician understands that
use of an algebraical system of expression implies
nothing in particular about the subject-matter,
- not even that it is of a quantitative character.
But three things recommend such a system:
first, it is free from ambiguities and unrecognized
prejudices; second, it thoroughly analyzes meanings
and expresses them with great precision; and
third, it is readily handled by the mind and
facilitates that mental experimentation in
which all sound reasoning consists.
I desire from time to time when the editors of
the Bulletin can find room for a little padding,
to offer some remarks (less desultory, perhaps, than
they may seem) about the logic of mathematics;

and I will begin by considering the Boolian
calculus of logic.

This algebra marks the merit of propositions upon
a scale of {del}two{/del}. {add}Good and bad or{/add} True and false, are its only grades. It is
a system of quantity having but two values; and each of
its equations is, as it were, an arithmetical congruence having
2 for its modulus.
Most writers on the subject make the letters denote
classes of things, primarily, and propositions only seconda-
rily they follow the old logicians in taking the
categorical proposition to be more primitive than
the conditional and disjunctive propositions. At
first blush, this seems evident; for conditional
and disjunctive propositions are built up out of
other propositions. But a careful {del}ex{/del} examination
under the light of the logic of relatives shews that
every categorical proposition of a general nature,
such as alone can be fully expressed {del}onto{/del} {add}by{/add} the
ordinary conventions of language, affirms or

Thus, to say that all men are mortal, or whatsoever
is a man is mortal, amounts to saying that any

object, no matter how selected, if it be a man, then
is mortal. The only distinction between the categorical

I will begin these notes with some remarks about

It will be remarked that nothing in the formal definition
of “smaller than” distinguishes it from “greater than.” But the
second proposition excludes the possibility of a thing being at
once greater than and smaller than itself. In short “smaller
than” cannot be distinguished by a definition alone, but it
needs an index to distinguish it, just as right and left do.

If a fallacious argument is not stated {add}pretty fully,{/add} the logician
may be at his wits’ end to know what sort of a fallacy
to call it, because he cannot quite see {del}how{/del} {add}from what point of view{/add} anybody
could regard it as an argument at all. The “Achilles and the
Tortoise” is an illustration in point. It begins by insisting
that in order to overtake the tortoise Achilles must first
go as far as where the tortoise starts, next to where the
tortoise then is, next again to where the tortoise then is,
“and so on ad infinitum.” Granted, of course; but what
of it? Why, then it is {add}said to be{/add} impossible for Achilles to overtake
the tortoise, at all. Good gracious, what a jump! No sem-
blance of a {del}[illegible]{/del} {add}proof{/add} is given for it, and {del}what{/del} how people
{del}think{/del} there is rhyme or reason in it it is difficult to
{del}fancy{/del} imagine.
Is it that {add}the performance of{/add} an endless series of actions is deemed too
fatiguing?
Is it that the “so on ad infinitum” means that it is
supposed Achilles has to do something analogous to an infinite

number of sums in addition?  Naturally, the distinction
between addition proper and the evaluation of the limit
of an infinite series whose general term is given would
be overlooked.

necessarily infinite?  If one had asked him why he
thought so, would he have said with Full-Private James,
        "If glimmering thought occurs to me,

Or would he have responded that the sum of a series of
unequal positive numbers {del}is greater{/del} exceeds the product
of the number of terms by into the value of the least term?
If so, the obvious answer would be that in {add}a{/add} {del}endless{/del} series
of quantities each smaller than the preceding, the least
is the last; but an endless series has {del}by ex vi termini{/del}
no last, and consequently no least.

The doctrine of limits is a pretty thing. In its best form,
it {del}[illegible]{/del} rests upon the hypothesis of a system of
numbers from which infinitesimals are excluded. {del}Every{/del} All
mathematical reasoning consists simply in tracing out
the consequences of hypotheses. It is a familiar result
to find the hypothesis self-contradictory. But attempts
have been made to apply the method of limits to show
the absurdity of the idea of infinitesimals. Is it not a
strange logical procedure to deduce as a consequence
of one hypothesis that a conflicting hypothesis is untrue?
Of course, it is untrue if the first one remains true; but how is
a mere hypothesis to decide that it cannot itself be set aside?

We now perfectly comprehend the order of arrangement of the real num-
bers. We can imagine points to be so arranged, or instants of times, or anything
• {del}else{/del} which we can imagine to be arranged in a row that is not sparse. We
commonly imagine peacocks to be so arranged, but we can comprehend
what such arrangement would be. The only difficulty about the ima-
• gination is that we cannot picture a row of peacocks in which
each peacock did not have a next neighbor. There is no such diffi-
culty in conceiving that the objects which are so arranged, instead

An important question in formal logic is whether a hypothetical
proposition is essentially the same as a categorical proposition, or
not. I maintain that it is so, that not only can a hypothetical
be expressed as a categorical, but also that conversely there is
no {add}logical{/add} species of categorical which is not logically equivalent to
some species of hypothetical.

In {del}denying{/del} this, I {del}[unclear: suppose] that{/del} {add}do not mean{/add} hypotheticals {del}are not{/del} {add}in the sense in which they are{/add} confined
to propositions of the form “If A is true, then B is true,” {del}but{/del} {add}mean{/add} that
{add}the class is{/add} {del}they are{/del} to be extended, as it generally was in most ages, though
not usually since Kant, to all propositions {del}expressing{/del} {add}asserting{/add} relations
between problematical propositions. For example, “It does not
follow {del}that{/del} because A is true that B is necessarily true;” “{del}It [unclear: does]{/del}
the government could be ridiculed with impunity that would
necessarily tend to bring about a license to attack the rights
of property;” “That I should {add}prefer to{/add} be killed in a duel {del}would be preferable{/del} {add}rather than to be{/add}
{del}being{/del} dishonored in the eyes of Society.” All these are hypotheti-
cal propositions in the sense defined.

In the first place every hypothetical has to do with a
range of possibilities. That is possible which in an assumed
state of information is not known to be false. The state
of information may be the actual state of the speaker or it
may be some imaginary state in which either more or
(more usually) less is assumed to be known than actually
is known. This gives rise to the various kinds of possibility.

Ms. 819

                The Conception of Infinity

The syllogism of transposed quantity is a kind
of reasoning, so named by De Morgan, who
first called attention to it, of which the fol-
lowing is an example.

Every dollar which an insurance company
pays for losses must have been paid in
for premiums (including {del}the{/del} {add}accrued{/add} interest on them)
or else the capital (including accrued interest)
is impaired. Suppose, then, that, considering
all the new property {del}[illegible]{/del} insured in one
year, if the total losses which ever happens
on this property exceed the total premiums
paid on it, and suppose this is true for the
new property insured every year. It follows
that the capital must be impaired.

De Morgan considered this reasoning as
absolutely valid; and because he was an
actuary, I have selected an example from

his professor which the actuaries of his
day would have considered sound reasoning,
but which the actuaries of our day would
laugh at. I give another example, which
shows more clearly the essence of the
reasoning. It is given, with a satirical pur-
pose, by Balzac, in the preface or {del}introduc-{/del}
{del}tion to the{/del} Physiologie du mariage. It runs
something like this:
Every young Frenchman (I believe what
he says) seduces a Frenchwoman; But no
Frenchwoman is seduced but once, and for
each Frenchwoman, you can name a distinct
Frenchmen (i.e. there are as many Frenchmen
as Frenchwomen;) Hence, every Frenchwoman
must get seduced.
There are some collections for which
such reasoning holds good, and others
for which it does not hold good. The

reasoning about insurance takes no account
of the continual increase of business, in
consequence of which it is not the question
whether the property destroyed paid fully its
losses, but whether the new premiums are
continually sufficient to pay losses on the
property formerly insured. (Whether this will
be a sound principle in the end, is a question
of whether this will always be kept up. The
insurance men say it will work till there is
such a universal crash that they will fail
when everybody fails.) The argument of Balzac
overlooks the fact that the seduced are on the
average younger than the seducers, and this
vitiates the argument to the extent of the
increase of population in that time,
Here is another example of this reasoning.
In every count of a certain collection of things
every number used in the counting is {add}immediately{/add} followed
by a number used in the counting, or else

there is a maximum number used. But no
number is {del}preceded{/del}{add}immediately{/add} preceded by two different
numbers; and there is a number used, namely
one, which is not preceded by any number.
Hence, there is a maximum number used.
To say there is a maximum number used
in a collection, is to say that that
collection is finite, or to avoid {del}an{/del}{add}a much{/add} abused
word, let us say enumerable. We thus see
that the general character of an enumerable
collection is that, to it, the syllogisms of
transposed quantity are truly applicable. We
also see that there are collections of which this
is not true, such as the whole collection of
positive integer numbers; for there is no
highest number. There is thus no difficulty in
conceiving that a collection is nonenumerable.
Fermat introduced a new method of
reasoning about numbers. Suppose a certain
property, P, is proved to belong, if to any

number [unclear: N?] then to the next higher N+1, then,
this property if it belongs to the first number
1, belongs to all numbers. This kind of
reasoning I call the Fermatian inference.

It holds good of all enumerable collections,
no matter in what order their members be
arranged. It holds good of some innumerable
collections, provided they are properly arranged.
It would not do to arrange all the odd numbers
first with all the even numbers following them.
It is necessary to arrange the objects in such a
linear order that each one is preceded by
an enumerable collection of objects, or by
none.

It is susceptible of demonstration that there
are {del}collections{/del} with which we frequently
deal which cannot be so arranged as to render
the Fermatian inference truly applicable. In
other words, the positive integer numbers cannot
be assigned each to a different member
of such a collection.

Theorem of the Fermatian inference

Rules Let Q be {del}such{/del} a relation such that
nothing has two Qs. Then I say no two
different things are Q to themselves.
{del}Proof{/del} Let V be something Q to itself
and W be   "    "    " .
I have to prove V and W identical.
Let κ be such a character that to say anything
has it is the same as to say that any character whatever
which is true of anything then of every Q of that
thing and which is true of A is also true of V
Then I say if anything has the character κ, every
thing Q of A has the same character.
For if not, let B be something Q of A which has
not the character κ. That is to say, there is some character
δ such that belongs to B, and which belongs to every-
thing which is Q to a thing it belongs to, and yet
does not belong to V.
Now let the character σ consist in either possessing δ
or being A, Of course A has the character σ.
A thing Q to a thing having the character σ

m s 826

[diagram: circled “5” or “S” at upper right]

is either Q to a thing having the character 𝛿
or is Q of A. But B, the only thing Q of A
has the character 𝛿 and everything Q of a
thing having the character 𝛿 has the
character 𝛿. Hence, everything Q of a
thing having the character σ has the
character 𝛿 and consequently the character
σ. Since therefore A has the character σ
which also belongs to everything the Q of anything
to which it belongs. Then if A has the character
κ, σ is true of V. κ is also true of V; hence
if κ is not true of B, V and B are not identical
Since then, B is Q of A. V is not Q of A since
nothing has two Qs and consequently A and
V are not the same, for V is Q of V. Since then
V has the character σ but {del}has{/del} not A it has
the character 𝛿. Thus the supposition that
there is a character that belongs to B (or the Q of A)
and belongs to everything Q of a thing to which
it belongs and yet does not belong to V
is reduced to absurdity. In other words if

[diagram: small circled/elliptical figure at upper right, marked “C”]

A
anything has the character κ, the Q of A has
the same character.

Now if W has any character which belongs
to the Q of whatever it belongs to, everything
in the universe {add}which is{/add} considered has that character,
as no thing by definition, V has the character
κ. Since then κ is a character which belongs
to the Q of whatever it belongs to, by the
Fermatian inference everything in the universe
has the character κ. Therefore W has the
character κ.

Consider then the character which consists
{del}as one{/del} in being W {add}but{/add} not V. This is a character
which belongs to W, unless W and V are identical,
And if it belongs to anything (which can only be W) it
belongs to the Q of that thing (for the Q of W is W).
Hence, since the character κ belongs to
W, unless W and V are identical the
character of being W but not V belongs to
V, which is absurd. Hence, W and V cannot
be identical. Q.E.D.

§1
Der Mächtigkeitsbegriff oder die Cardinalzahl.

He defines a ‘Menge’, that is, a Collection, as follows:
"jede Zusammenfassung M von bestimmten wohlunter-
schiedenen Objecten m unsrer Anschauung oder unseres
Denkens zu einem Ganzen,"
This is remarkably accurate. No need of the subjectivism
of "unsrer Anschauung oder unseres Denkens." He notes
that the members which he calls ‘Elemente’ are {del}singular{/del}
definite, {add}‘bestimmten’{/add} but not that they are also {del}singular{/del}{add}individual{/add}, and that
they are independent, ‘wohlunterschiedenen’. But he
does not {del}again{/del} analyse that character, and say
in what it consists. He implies that the relation of
the collection to a member is that of inclusion
but this he does not analyse.

He denotes this thus
(1)                         M = {m}
The aggregate (Vereinigung) of several collections
M, N, P, which have no common members he writes

The members of this collection are the members of the col-
lections M, N, P, etc.
Part “Theil” or “Theilmenge” is a different (anderer)
collection whose members are all members of the whole.
A part of a part is a part.
Every collection has a “Multitude” “Mächtigkeit” which
he also calls Cardinalzahl. He ought not to confound them.
He defines multitude in the German fashion by
negation. I think this is idle business

hand?) dadurch aus der Menge M hervorgeht, dass

m und von der Ordnung ihres Gegebenseins abstrahirt
wird.
This is bad. He ought first to define the relation
of greater and less and show if possible that of
any two collections both cannot be greater than
the other.

Hamilton & Mansell.                                      Ms.822

1. The Laws of Reasoning are laws of consciousness.
2. These laws apply to all such things as
four-sided triangles, square roots of minus, infinite
lines &c.
For if it did not apply we could neither pronounce
any of these to be non-existent nor could we in-
troduce them into a train of reasoning; far less base
conclusions respecting the physical universe on them.
3. In short, the laws of reasoning apply not merely
to that which is conceived but to all which we
can suppose {add}to be a{/add} conception. They therefore apply
to all those things which we can try to conceive,
at least, to all those whose elements are conceptions
which do not however admit in conception of be-
ing combined.
4. Mansell admits the following definitions
The Absolute is that which is not dependent (without
necessary relation)
The Infinite is that which is not limited.
5 Mansell says there is a contradiction in supposing
the Absolute Infinite to exist, or vice versa.
He means of course
that correct reasoning applied to this leads to contra-
diction

But we cannot conceive the infinite.

II

The Proposition that infinitely small quantities can be
neglected in reasoning, may be resolved into two viz:
1^st = They may be neglected in the conclusion.
This is true if a proposition whose, {add}absolute{/add} error is infinitely
small {del}[illegible]{/del} may be taken as true. Now if the error
is infinitely small we cannot conceive of the difference
between that proposition & absolute truth. But propositions
are only conceptions, and two conceptions which do not dif-
fer in conception do not differ at all.
2^nd They may be neglected in the premises, so long as
not cancelled by infinitely large quantities.
This follows from the fact that any number of finite
decrements of their infinity will never leave them finite

III

'Ms. 823

The writers of {del}treatises{/del} {add}logic-books with some exceptions
& with very few{/add}
-
are themselves but shamb{unclear: ling?} reasoners. How
will those say to that Goethe, Let me cast out the
mote out of thine eye; and lo, the beam is in
thine own eye? In fact one may say of
philosophers at large, both small and great, that
their reasoning {add}mostly{/add} is so {add}loose and fallacious{/add} {del}[unclear: fallacious]{/del} that it would be
derided in mathematics, in political-economy,
and in physical science. I felicitate myself that
I was made to see this at an early age; for even
in my teens, when I was reading Kant, Spinoza,
and Hegel, my father, who was the celebrated
mathematician Benjamin Peirce, not a power-
ful analyst of thought, so that his demonstrations
were sometimes faulty, but a mind who never
once failed, as well as I can remember, to draw the
correct conclusion from given premises, unless
by a mere slip, my father, I say, would induce
me to tell him about the proofs offered by the
philosophers, and in a very few words would almost

invariably rip them up and throw them empty.
He had even less mercy for such philosophers
as Hobbes {add}Hume,{/add} {del}and{/del} James Mill. In this way, the
bad habit of thinking {del}[illegible]{/del} which would have been
imposed upon me by these mighty powers
were in great measure, though I confess not
entirely, overcome. Would that every young
student of philosophy could enjoy a similar
companionship with a stalwart practical reasoner,

How often have I heard it said that the
study of philosophy requires hard thinking! Posh!
I tell my {del}students{/del} scholars That hard thinking is to
{add}That was the way I used to make my own boys
youths.{/add}
be shunned like the pest. In mathematics the com-
plexity of the problems makes it often a little difficult
to hold all the different elements of our {del}the{/del} {add}imaginations{/add}
in their right places. In {del}one{/del} {add}a certain{/add} sense, therefore, the hard
thinking is occasionally requisite in that discipline.
But philosophy does not present any such
complications, and there is no work for
hard thinking to do. What is wanted above all,
in metaphysics, or general philosophy, is

thorough and mature thinking; and what is more
particularly requisite in the critic of arguments
is exact thinking.

In order to maintain the sweeping charges which
I make against logicians, I will begin be exami-
ning some statements by Mansell. Now, Mansell
is certainly a {add}considerable{/add} figure in the logical world. He
represents, it is true, the most degraded of all
schools of logic, a school which severed all
connection between logic and real thought, by making
{del}the{/del} it treat of the forms of thought, and of pure
thought, at that. It, thus, {del}is{/del} really converted logic
into a branch of pure mathematics, but having no
mathematical interest on account of its extreme
simplicity. But, notwithstanding this simplicity,
that school continues to boggle the mathematicians,
and good deal of it they quite falsified. This
extra-ordinary doctrine is not yet quite gone
out of vogue; for I see that the latest jumble
of logical pickings, published in New York this

your, has a chapter on the quantification of the
predicate. But notwithstanding this {del}wretchedly{/del}
idle and weak position, Mansell was [unclear: aible?],
not a dolt, and was as superior to the small
fry whom I might name, - to such a man as
{add}for example,{/add}
Forbes, to select of their best men, and one who
is not living, - as possible. Now Mansell states
that the validity of affirmative syllogism has for
its necessary and sufficient condition the princi-
ple of identity, “A is A.” While negative syllogisms
depend upon the principle of contradiction, “A is not
non-A,” and the principle of excluded middle, “What
is not-non-A is A.” Mansell was not, it is true,
the first logician by any means to make these state-
ments, but he makes himself fully responsible
for them. The notion that “A is A” is the principle
of syllogism must be a fruit of “loose thinking.”
In a certain state of mental vacancy induced by
self-hypnotization the formula “A is A” probably appears

items.title: On Gravity as an Index of the Movements of the Earth's Crust

pages.t:
No. 1088

On Gravity as an Index of the movements
of the Earth's crust.

It is shown mathematically that, whatever
the internal constitution of the earth, where
gravity, after correction for latitude and ele-
vation, is in excess, there is a force tending
to depress the surface, at the expense of the
elevation of other parts, and vice versa. It
is shown that this {del}force{/del} pressure is so great
that where the excess or deficiency of gravity
exceeds the errors of observation, it cannot be
supposed that any material could resist the
strain; and consequently, elevation or depres-
sion must in these cases be going on. A list
of sinking and rising regions is given. The most
remarkable region of depression is in the Paci-
fic Ocean, in the western part especially. One
of the most marked regions of rising is the
German ocean.

§1  Josephus says the hin was equal to 2 {del}Attic{/del} χόες.
Now 1 χοῦς = 6 ξέσται = 12 κοτύλαι. I follow Attics in
making it 3.03 litres. The measures found in Naxos (Dumori,
Revue archéologique 1873. vol xxvi. p43) {add}seem to{/add} make the χοῦς a little
less than this or say 3 litres. But this is not {add}quite{/add} certain. I make
it from these measures 2.96 litres. The hin would thus be
6 litres, and the Bath 36 litres.
§2  Josephus also says the gomor was 7 Attic κοτύλαι.
This would be 1.75 litres, or about 1/2 what would be expected
from §1. If we knew the explanation of this difficulty,
this passage would probably make the Bath 35 litres.
§3. Josephus says the sath = 1½ modius Italicus. Now
There was a wet and a dry modius Italicus, one is 1½
times the other. Epiphanius quoting Josephus makes
him say the sath is one modius Italicus. He therefore
understands Josephus to mean {del}the{/del} 1½ of the smaller modius.
The small modius Italicus is 16 sextarii the larger 24 sextarii.
The Farnese congius contains, according to Hase, 3.387 litres.
As this is 6 sextarii, the sath would be 13.547 litres {add}and the bath{/add} would
be 40.64 litres. But modern writers make the congius
3.186 litres, making the value only 38.33 litres.
§4. Josephus makes two other statements which are unintel-
ligible as they stand, and it is unnecessary to resort to con-
jectural modifications.
§5. Epiphanius always says the bath is 50 sextarii, and
Queipo is wrong in saying he ever gives any other
figure. This is, {add}above{/add} 1/3 the value assigned by Josephus
to the ephah. It is 20.22 litres by the Farnese congius
and 26.55 litres according to modern writers.
§6. The comparison of 1 Kings vii 26 with 2 Chron iv 5
leads us to infer there were two baths, one 2/3 of the
other.

§8. Epiphanius says that the [unclear: commerce]
q ξέστας, that is sextarii. But the Latin translation
changes this to 15 or 16, which latter number is given
by Epiphanius in another place. There seem, there-
fore, to have been more than 2 hins. This would
make 54 {del}hins{/del} sextarii to the bath. If we suppose it
really contained 52 sextarii, he might roughly say it
contained 50 and on dividing by 6 he would get 9.
But this does not seem satisfactory, because he is
generally more exact. The statement as it stands would make the bath 30.5
litres by the Farnese congius, 28.7 litres by modern metres.
§9. Epiphanius says the sextus was a Hebrew measure,
and contained 1/4 Hebrew modius. He also says that
it contained 1 1/2 Roman modius or 24 sextarii. According
to this, 1/5 should be subtracted from the values of §3,
making 32.51 litres by the {del}Roman{/del} Farnese congius and
30.66 by modern metres.
§10. The Septuagint makes the Hebrew cor = 6 Egyptian
Artabs. Taking the great Artab at 55.16 litres, the
small make the bath 21.71 litres. This agrees with
Epiphanius, cited by Eusebius, and others.
§11. Solomon's molten sea is said by Josephus to
have been strictly hemispherical, and is said to
have been 10 cubits in diameter and to have held
2000 baths according to Kings, 3000 according to
Chronicles. Taking the sacred cubit as 0.525 metre,
the solid contents would be 37900 litres, making
or 18 litres or 12.6 litres to the bath.

{add}Bath.{/add}

Hebrew liquid measure = 72 Logs = 6 hins = 1/10 cor.
It corresponds to the dry measure the ephah = 72 logs
= 18 cabs = 10 gomers = 3 seahs = 1/10 cor.

A. There seems to have been a bath of about 36 litres.
For,
1st Josephus says the hin was equal to two Attic χόες.
Aineas makes the χοῦς 3.03 litres. The measures found
at Naxos make it 2.96 litres. Call it 3 litres. It follows
that the bath was 36 litres.
2nd Josephus says the Hebrew cor contains 10 Attic
medimni. Now the medimnus is 16 χόες or 48 litres.
If we suppose he meant metretas, the {del}bath would would{/del}
{add}statement{/add}
agree with the other.
3rd. Solemon's molten sea is said by Josephus to have
been strictly hemispherical, and according to 1 Kings vii
it was 10 cubits in diameter and contained {del}2000{/del} baths.
Taking the sacred cubit at 0.525 metre (according to
the Egyptian cubits which are preserved) the total contents
of the sphere would be
        4/3 π R³ = 4/3 × 3 1/7 × {del}9.145{/del} 18.09 = 75.8 metre cubes
and 1/2000 part of this would be 37.9 litres to the bath.
4th. Josephus also says the gomer was 7 Attic κοτύλαι.
This would make the bath only 17.5 litres, which doubled
would be 35 litres.
5th. The salt was a heaped measure, so that all state-
ments about it are approximate and doubtful. Various
writers, Josephus, Epiphanius, etc say that it was 1 1/2
medius or 24 sextarii. This would make the ephah
(and presumably the bath) 72 sextarii. The Farnese
congius of 6 sextarii contains according to Hase
3.387 litres. Hence the sext. would be 13.547 litres.
But according to Epiphanius the heaping increases
it by 1/4 part. This would be 2.71 litres of heaping,
a very large allowance. Making this allowance and
multiplying, the ephah would be 33 1/2 litres. With
a little smaller allowance for heaping it would be
36.

5. There seems also to have been.
12. Epiphanius always says the Bath is 50 sextarii, which
would be 28.2 litres by the Farnese congius.
2nd Chronicles iv. 5 compared with 1 Kings vii 26 seems
to indicate a second bath about 2/3 of the first, or about
24 litres.
3rd. Josephus says 70 cors of flour make 41 baths
medicine. Supposing he means to say 7 cors or
70 ephahs, this would make the epha 28 litres.

Determinations of the Lengths of Decimetre
Scales Nos. 3, 4, and 5.

Starting with Metre No 49, we have the comparisons of this
with the metre des Archives, and the discussion by Foerster.
The coefficient of expansion has been determined
by me with a high degree of precision. See Gravity at Initial
Stations.
Glass metre scale {del}[illegible]{/del} No 1, was compared on the Blair com-
parator with No. 49. Namely, 1884 Dec 20-22 ten times
at temperatures about 8°.5 C., and 1884 July 1-27 ten times
at temperatures about 23°.5 C, and by my okchet twice 1884
Dec. 29 at about 29°. The intervals between the different lines
of {del}them{/del} Glass metre scale No 1 were measured by Thomas-
meter A of Blair comparator 1884 Dec 30. Besides this,
Glass metre scale No 1 was compared with Metre No 49
on the Saxon slope comparator 1881 June 20-27, at tempera-
tures from 73° F to 76° F.
Glass decimetre No 3 was compared {add}on the{/add} Blair com-
parator with all the decimetres of glass metre scale
No 1, 1884 Oct 21 - Dec 9, six times at temperatures ranging
from 12°.5 C to 20° C. Besides this, Glass decimetre No. 3 was
compared on my micrometric comparator 1879 May and June
with {del}[illegible]{/del} all the decimetres of glass metre scale No 1 at tem-
peratures from 68° to 75°. But as my comparator was not
adapted to supporting so long a bar, I attribute little weight
to these comparisons. Further Decimetre No 3 was compared
1878 May 28 with Schott's brass decimetre at 54°.7 C. For
the coefficient of expansion see below.
Glass decimetre No 4 was compared with extraordin-
ary precision on my micrometric comparator with
glass decimetre No 3, first 1879 July 14-31 at tempera-
tures from 70° F to 84° F, and then 1880 Jan 30-Feb 9 at tempe-
ratures from 40° F to 49° F. The relative coefficient of expan-
sion between the two is also thus determined.
Glass decimetre No 5 was compared in the Blair compa-
rator with all the decimetres of glass metre scale No 1, 1884
Sep 12 - Oct 9 at about 22° C, six times. Coefficient of
expansion unknown.

The coefficients of expansion rest at present on the
following data.

lines is nearly erased. Its length is counted
from the fourth from the end to the
middle one of the three at the {add}other{/add} {del}[illegible]{/del}
end.

{del}points;{/del}

p₁, and p₂, The {add}statical{/add} displacements which those
same forces {del}produce{/del} {add}communicate{/add} each to the other Knife-
middle point of the other Knife-edge;
T₁, and T₂, the natural periods of os-
cillation of the two pendulums;
T′₁ and T′₂, the periods of oscillation
of the two pendulums when each is swung
alone on the given support.

If horizontal forces f₁ and f₂ be applied
to the two Knife edges, the displacements
{del}produced{/del} will be
        S₁ = f₁p₁ + f₂q₂
        S₂ = f₂p₂ + f₁q₁.
These give
        f₁ = 1/(p₁p₂−q₁q₂) (p₂S₁ + q₂S₂)
        f₂ = 1/(p₁p₂−q₁q₂) (p₁S₂ − q₁S₁).

The work done will be
S₁f₁ + S₂f₂ = 1/(p₁p₂−q₁q₂) (p₂S₁² − (q₁+q₂)S₁S₂ + p₁S₂²).

Hence, the potential energy of a situation
{del}will be{/del} defined by φ₁, φ₂, S₁ and S₂ will be

−M₁g₁h₁ cosφ₁ − M₂g₂h₂ cosφ₂ + (p₂s₁² − (q₁+q₂)s₁s₂ + p₁s₂²)/(p₁p₂ − q₁q₂)

The actual energy, as I have shown in my first
paper on the flexure of a pendulum support,
will be

1/2 M₁l₁h₁(Dₜφ₁)² + M₁h₁ cosφ₁(Dₜφ₁)(Dₜs₁) + 1/2 M₁(Dₜs₁)²
+ 1/2 M₂l₂h₂(Dₜφ₂)² + M₂h₂ cosφ₂(Dₜφ₂)(Dₜs₂) + 1/2 M₂(Dₜs₂)²,

The canonical equations of Lagrange are
then as follows:

l₁Dₜ²φ₁ + cosφ₁·Dₜ²s₁ + sinφ₁·Dₜφ₁·Dₜs₁ = −g₁ sinφ₁
l₂Dₜ²φ₂ + cosφ₂·Dₜ²s₂ + sinφ₂·Dₜφ₂·Dₜs₂ = −g₂ sinφ₂
M₁h₁ cosφ₁·Dₜ²φ₁ − M₁h₁ sinφ₁·(Dₜφ₁)² + M₁Dₜ²s₁ = (−2p₁s₁ + (q₁+q₂)s₂)/(p₁p₂−q₁q₂)
M₂h₂ cosφ₂·Dₜ²φ₂ − M₂h₂ sinφ₂·(Dₜφ₂)² + M₂Dₜ²s₂ = (−2p₂s₂ + (q₁+q₂)s₁)/(p₁p₂−q₁q₂)

or, omitting terms of the second degree

{del}l₁²D{/del}  l₁Dₜ²φ₁ + Dₜ²s₁ = −g₁φ₁
l₂Dₜ²φ₂ + Dₜ²s₂ = −g₂φ₂
M₁h₁Dₜ²φ₁ + M₁Dₜ²s₁ = (−2p₁s₁ + (q₁+q₂)s₂)/(p₁p₂−q₁q₂)
M₂h₂Dₜ²φ₂ + M₂Dₜ²s₂ = (−2p₂s₂ + (q₁+q₂)s₁)/(p₁p₂−q₁q₂)

Omitting, in the third and fourth equations,
the small terms in D_t²s₁, and D_t²s₂ and
also there {del}[illegible]{/del} substituting
        D_t²φ₁ = - 1/T₁² φ₁
        D_t²φ₂ = - 1/T₂² φ₂

we have
        - M₁h₁/T₁² φ₁ = [-2p₁s₁ + (q₁+q₂)s₂] / [p₁p₂ - q₁q₂]

        - M₂h₂/T₂² φ₂ = [-2p₂s₂ + (q₁+q₂)s₁] / [p₁p₂ - q₁q₂] .

These give

        s₁ = [p₁p₂ - q₁q₂] / [4p₁p₂ - (q₁+q₂)²] { 2p₂ M₁h₁/T₁² φ₁ + (q₁+q₂) M₂h₂/T₂² φ₂ }

        s₂ = [p₁p₂ - q₁q₂] / [4p₁p₂ - (q₁+q₂)²] { 2p₁ M₂h₂/T₂² φ₂ + (q₁+q₂) M₁h₁/T₁² φ₁ } .

Substituting these values in the first two
canonical equations, we have
        T₁′² D_t² φ₁ - [formula: see image] (q₁+q₂) M₂h₂/l₂ · g₂/g₁ D_t² φ₂ = - φ₁

        T₂′² D_t² φ₂ - [formula: see image] (q₁+q₂) M₁h₁/l₁ · g₁/g₂ D_t² φ₁ = - φ₂.

If we write

        ψ₁ = g₁ √(μ₁h₁/ρ₁) · φ₁

        ψ₂ = g₂ √(μ₂h₂/ρ₂) · φ₂

and if we put

        ΣT² = T₁′² + T₂′²
        ΔT² = T₁′² - T₂′²

        Q = (ρ₁ρ₂ - g₁g₂)/(2ρ₁ρ₂ - 1/2(g₁+g₂)²) (g₁+g₂) √(μ₁μ₂h₁h₂/ρ₁ρ₂)

these equations take the form

        (ΣT² + ΔT²) Dₜ²ψ₁ + QDₜ²ψ₂ = -2ψ₁

        (ΣT² - ΔT²) Dₜ²ψ₂ + QDₜ²ψ₁ = -2ψ₂

Let us now take

        u = √(√((ΔT²)² + Q²) + ΔT²) · ψ₁ + √(√((ΔT²)² + Q²) - ΔT²) · ψ₂
        v = √(√((ΔT²)² + Q²) - ΔT²) · ψ₁ - √(√((ΔT²)² + Q²) + ΔT²) · ψ₂

so that

        ψ₁ = √(√((ΔT²)² + Q²) + ΔT²)/(2√((ΔT²)² + Q²)) · u
             + √(√((ΔT²)² + Q²) - ΔT²)/(2√((ΔT²)² + Q²)) · v

        ψ₂ = √(√((ΔT²)² + Q²) - ΔT²)/(2√((ΔT²)² + Q²)) · u
             - √(√((ΔT²)² + Q²) + ΔT²)/(2√((ΔT²)² + Q²)) · v.

items.title: Note on the Theory of the Economy of Research

pages.t:
Copied
AD.
June 4.
'77

Mss 1093                                      [unclear: Appendix Reprint] 76     (Notes and copy
                                                                           retained by E.H.)        1
Note on the Theory of the Economy of Research. By Assist. C. S. Peirce.

[diagram: boxed notation “7 C 20 #C”]

        When a research is of a quantitative nature, the progress of

it is marked by the diminution of the probable error. The results

of non-quantitative researches also have an inexactitude or inde-

terminacy which is analogous to the probable error of quantitative

determination. To this inexactitude, although it be not numeri-

cally expressed, the term probable error may be conveniently

extended.

        The doctrine of Economy, in general, treats of the relations

between utility and cost. That branch of it which relates to

research considers the relations between the utility and the

cost of diminutions of the probable error of our Knowledge. Its

main problem is how with a given expenditure of money,

time, and energy, to obtain the most valuable {del}addition{/del}

to our Knowledge.

        Let r denote the probable error of any result; and write

s = 1/r. Let U_r . dr denote the infinitesimal utility of any in-

finitesimal diminution, dr, of r. Let V_s . ds denote the infin-

tesimal cost of any infinitesimal increase, ds, of s. Let subscrip

{del}letters{/del} be attached to z, s, U, and V, to distinguish their
different problems into which investigations are made. Then
the total cost of any series of researches will be

Σᵢ ∫ Vᵢ sᵢ . dsᵢ ;

and their total utility will be

Σᵢ ∫ Uᵢ rᵢ . drᵢ .

The problem will be to make the second {del}logical{/del} {add}expression{/add} a maximum by
varying the inferior limits of its integrations, on the condition
that the first expression remains of constant value.

Let us consider the general and usual properties of the func-
tions U and V:

And first as to the relation between the exactitude of know-
ledge and its utility. The utility of knowledge consists in its
capability of being combined with other knowledge so as to enable
us to calculate {del}what we should do{/del} how we should act. If the
knowledge is {del}more{/del} uncertain we are obliged to do more than
is necessary in order to cover this uncertainty. And thus
the utility of any {add}increase of{/add} knowledge is measured by the amount
of wasted effort it saves us multiplied by the specific cost
of that species of effort. Now we know from the theory
of errors that the uncertainty in the calculated amount of

effort necessary to the given forth, may be represented
by an expression of the form

        e √(a + r²),

where a and e are constants, {add}depending on,{/add}
And therefore, the differential coefficient of this multi-
plied by the specific cost of the effort in question, say k/e,
gives

        Uᵣ = k/e  r/√(a + r²).

When a is very small compared with r this becomes nearly
constant, and in the reverse case it is nearly proportional
to r. An analogous proposition must hold for non quanti-
tative research.

Let us next consider the relation between the exactitude
of a result and the cost of attaining it. When we increase our
exactitude by multiplying observations and {del}the cost of{/del} the different
observations are independent of one another as to their cost, we
Know from the theory of errors that ∫Vₛ.ds is proportional
to s² and that consequently Vₛ is proportional to s. If the costs
of the observations are not independent (which usually hap-
pens) the cost will not increase so fast relatively to the
accuracy; but if the errors of the observations are not
independent (which also usually happens) the cost will
increase faster relatively to the accuracy; and these two
perturbing influences may be supposed in the long run, to

balance one another. We may, therefore, take

        V_s = k s,

where k represents the specific cost of the investigation.

We thus see that when an investigation is commenced, after
the initial expenses are once paid, at little cost we improve our
knowledge; and that improvement is especially valuable; but,
as the investigation goes on, additions to our knowledge cost
more and more and at the same time are of less and less
{del}profit{/del}. Thus, when Chemistry sprang into being, Dr. Wollaston
with a few test tubes and phials {del}all contained{/del} on a tea-
tray, was able to make new discoveries of the greatest mo-
ment. Now, a thousand chemists, with the most elaborate
appliances, are not able, to reach results which are compa-
rable, in interest, with those early ones. All the sciences ex-
hibit the same phenomenon; and so does the course of life.
At first, we learn very easily, and the interest of experience
is very great; but {del}experience{/del}{add}it{/add} becomes harder and harder and
less and less worth while, until we are glad to have done,
{del}with it and{/del} with Life.

Let us now apply the expressions obtained for U_s and
V_s to the economic problem of research. The question is, having
certain means at our disposal, to which of two studies they should
be applied. The general answer is that we should study that
problem for which the economic urgency

→ Uᵣ . dᵣ / Vₛ . dₛ = r² Uᵣ / Vₛ = h/k . r⁴ / √(a + r²)        5


[unclear: K?] is a maximum. When the investigation has been carried to a certain
point this fraction will be reduced to the same value which it has
for another {add}research{/add} {del}question{/del}, and the two must then be carried on together,
until finally we shall be carrying on at once, researches into a
great number of questions, {del}the above fraction{/del} then with such re-
lative energies as to keep the fraction of equal values for all
of them. {del}As new probl{/del} When new and promising problems arise,
they should receive our attention to the exclusion of the old ones, un-
til their urgency becomes no greater than that of others. It
will be remembered that {del}the economic importance of{/del} our igno-
rance of a question, is a consideration which has between
three and four times the economic importance of either the
specific value of the solution or the specific cost of the inves-
tigation, in deciding upon its urgency.

Let us now consider the economic relations of different
researches to one another, 1st, as alternative methods of
reaching the same result, and 2nd, as {del}serving being useful{/del}
{del}only when their results are combined components of an expres-{/del}
{del}tion for something so as to deliver an ultimate conclusion{/del}
contributing different premises of the same argument.

Suppose we have two different methods of determining the
same quantity. Each of these methods is supposed to have an
accidental probable error and a constant probable error
so that the probable errors as derived from n observations
-- in the two ways are,
r₁′ = √(R₁² + S₁²/n)     and r₂′ = √(R₂² + S₂²/n).

The probable error of their weighted mean is

        1
    —————————————
    √(1/r₁′² + 1/r₂′²)

if their constant probable errors are thrown. The sole utility
of any observation of either is to reduce the error of the weighted
mean; hence,

Uᵣ₁ = -Dᵣ₁(r₁′⁻² + r₂′⁻²)^-1/2 = (r₁′⁻² + r₂′⁻²)^-3/2 r₁′⁻3.

And as the cost is proportional to the number of observations

V_S₁ = k₁ 1/Dₙ₁ S₁ = k₁/[Dₙ₁(R₁² + S₁²n⁻¹)^-1/2] = 2k₁ r₁′³ n₁²/S₁².

Hence the urgency is (omitting a {del}term{/del} {add}factor{/add} common to the values for the
two methods)

r₁′² Uᵣ₁/V_S₁ = 1/[k₁ S₁²(1 + r₁′/S₁² R₁²)²]

And as the urgency of the two methods ought to be the
same at the conclusion of the work we should have,

        √k₁ . s₁ (1 + n₁ R₁²/s₁²) = √k₂ . s₂ (1 + n₂ R₂²/s₂²),

which equation serves to determine the relative values of n₁,
and n₂. We again perceive that the cost is the smallest consi-
deration. The method which has the smallest accidental proba-
ble error is the one which is to be oftenest used in case only
a small number of observations are made,, but if a large
number are taken the method with the larger accidental
probable error is to be oftenest used, unless it has so much
greater a probable constant error as to countervail this
consideration. If one of the two methods has only 1/μ² th the
accidental probable error of the other, but costs μ² times as
much, the rule should be to make the {del}cost{/del} total cost of the
two methods {del}equal{/del} {add}inversely{/add} proportioned to the squares of their con-
stant errors.

Let us now consider the case in which two quantities
x₁ and x₂ are observed, the {del}known{/del} determination of which
serves only to determine a certain function of them, y. In
this case, the probable error of y is

        √(Dₓ₁y · x₁² + Dₓ₂y · x₂²)

and one shall have

Vx₁ = (dy/dx₁ x₁) /
√(dy/dx₁ x₁² + dy/dx₂ x₂²)

Vs₁ will have the same value as before; but neglecting now
the constant errors, one may write

Vs₁ = 2 k₁ S₁ n₁^½

Then the urgency (with omission of the common factor) is

S₁² / k₁ n₁³ · dy/dx₁

and as the two urgencies must be equal, we have

n₁/n₂ = S₁/S₂ √(k₂/k₁) √((dy/dx₁)/(dy/dx₂)) .

I propose now to make a practical application of these
formulae to {del}several{/del} {add}a{/add} problem connected with the determination
of the force of gravity; which, in fact, suggested the whole speculation.
The error in the observed duration of an oscillation of a
pendulum is due to three causes, 1^st the irregularity in the motion
of the pendulum, 2^nd the error of the comparison {del}of{/del} with the
clock, {del}and{/del} 3^rd the error in the rate of the clock. These three

on the supposition that the error is wholly caused by 10
jars. The units are 0.00000001 seconds.

                    Heavy end down
                                      [unclear: Pe.]    Calc.
Amplitude at beginning 2°; at end 1 1/2°.   330      342
   "        "       "     1 1/2°  "   "   1°.       254      244
   "        "       "     1°      "   "   1/2°.     150      170

                    Heavy end up.
Amplitude at beginning 2°; at end 1 1/2°.   728      714
